Title: How to Be a Pittsburgh Pirates Fan The Pittsburgh Pirates are a Major League Baseball (MLB) team based in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. Established in 1882, the Pirates have a rich history with numerous Hall of Fame players and six World Series titles. If you're interested in becoming a fan of this storied franchise, here is our comprehensive guide on how to be a devoted follower of the Pittsburgh Pirates. Step 1: Understand the History Learn about the Pirates' past successes and challenges. This includes understanding significant moments such as the "Bill Mazeroski Home Run" that won the 1960 World Series or Barry Bonds breaking Hank Aaron's home run record while playing for the Pirates in 1990. Knowing the highs and lows will give you an appreciation for what being a Pirate fan truly means. Key Tip: Research key figures like Honus Wagner, Roberto Clemente, Willie Stargell, and Andrew McCutchen who have had major impacts on the team. Step 2: Follow Current Players & Storylines Stay updated with current news surrounding the team by following official sources like pirates.com, mlb.com, or local newspapers like the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette. Keep track of player stats, trades, injuries, and other relevant information. Guideline: Use social media platforms such as Twitter or Instagram where many athletes and journalists share real-time updates. Step 3: Watch Games Regularly Watch as many games as possible during the season which runs from April through September/October. You can watch live broadcasts on AT&T SportsNet Pittsburgh if available in your area, or stream online via MLB.tv or other streaming services. Don't forget radio broadcasts on KDKA-FM! Key Tip: Even when busy, keeping up with game summaries or highlights later ensures you never miss crucial moments. Step 4: Attend Games at PNC Park If feasible, attend some games at PNC Park, considered one of baseball's most beautiful stadiums. The atmosphere at a live game cannot be replicated virtually. Cheer alongside fellow fans, enjoy ballpark food, and soak in the unique experience. Guideline: Plan ahead for tickets, parking, and tailgating. Check out promotional nights for additional fun. Step 5: Engage with Other Fans Join online communities, message boards, or local meetups for Pirates fans. Sharing opinions, debating strategies, and celebrating victories strengthens your bond with the team and its supporters. Key Tip: Always maintain respectful discourse; disagreement doesn't mean disrespect. Step 6: Show Your Support Visibly Purchase merchandise like jerseys, caps, shirts, etc., displaying the iconic black and gold colors. Representing the team publicly demonstrates pride and commitment. Guideline: Consider buying directly from the MLB Shop or officially licensed retailers to ensure authenticity and quality. Step 7: Participate in Traditions Participate in traditions observed by Pirates fans, whether it's singing "Take Me Out to the Ball Game" during the seventh-inning stretch, doing the wave, or shouting "Here We Go Steelers!" after a Pirates win - yes, really! Remember, being a true fan goes beyond merely cheering for wins; it involves appreciating the journey, embracing tradition, connecting with others, and staying informed. Following these steps will help turn you into a dedicated member of Bucco Nation. Um I think that definitely caught her attention. Not that she doesn't like anything like she XXX. Title: Convincing Your Parents to Get a Kitten or Cat Introduction: Getting a kitten or cat can be an exciting prospect, but convincing your parents might seem like a daunting task. This guide will provide you with comprehensive steps and useful tips to help increase your chances of success. Remember that every family is different, so tailor these suggestions according to your specific situation. Patience, understanding, and respect are crucial throughout this process. Step 1: Research Cats and Their Needs Before approaching your parents, gather information about cats and their needs. Learn about different breeds, typical behaviors, lifespans, and care requirements. Demonstrating knowledge about cats shows initiative and responsibility. Key points to research include: - Lifespan (indoor vs outdoor) - Breed characteristics (size, temperament, grooming needs) - Basic costs (food, litter, vet visits) - Care requirements (feeding schedules, exercise, playtime) Step 2: Evaluate Family Allergies and Schedules Consider whether anyone in your household has allergies or if there are any scheduling conflicts that could impact caring for a cat. If someone has allergies, look into hypoallergenic breeds or alternative solutions. For scheduling issues, think about ways to divide responsibilities among family members. Addressing potential concerns upfront shows consideration and maturity. Step 3: Prepare a Written Proposal Create a well-structured proposal outlining your case for getting a kitten or cat. Organize your thoughts using headings such as Benefits, Responsibility, Cost, and Solutions to Potential Issues. Be honest, clear, and concise while emphasizing positive aspects of having a pet cat. Use visual elements like charts or graphs to illustrate points like cost comparisons between various pets. Step 4: Discuss with Your Parents Choose an appropriate time and place to discuss your proposal with your parents. Make sure they're calm and receptive. Present your written proposal and explain its contents clearly. Listen carefully to their questions, comments, and concerns. Maintain composure even when faced with disagreement; showing emotional stability during discussions bodes well for demonstrating responsible ownership. Step 5: Negotiate Terms and Conditions If your parents express reservations, work together to find compromises. Offer solutions to alleviate fears or obstacles. Examples include agreeing to cover some expenses yourself, promising to handle certain tasks, or adjusting your request based on feedback (e.g., adopting an older cat instead of a kitten). Compromise shows flexibility and willingness to share responsibility. Key Tips: - Show genuine interest in taking care of the new pet - Highlight the benefits of having a cat, such as companionship, stress relief, teaching children responsibility, etc. - Emphasize personal growth opportunities through learning valuable life skills - Explore local shelters, rescues, or reputable breeders rather than buying from pet stores - Provide reassurance by sharing testimonials, articles, or videos showcasing happy families with cats Guidelines: - Always approach conversations with respect and patience - Avoid being confrontational or dismissive of parental concerns - Never resort to manipulation tactics like hiding a cat in the house before discussing it - Recognize that ultimately, the decision lies with your parents - Express gratitude regardless of their final answer, acknowledging their effort to consider your wishes Hers is on another piece of paper. Learn to talk like a sailor! Many words and phrases we say today actually come from expressions related to ships and the sea. First rate: A ship that had 100 guns or more and had three decks. Today we say something is "first rate" if it is of high quality. Learning the ropes: Ships had hundreds of ropes and it took sailors awhile to learn how each of them worked. To say that someone is "learning the ropes" means that they might be new to a job. Tidy: The ocean's tides, or times when the level of the waves rises and falls, are predictable. A "tidy" person is someone who is neat and orderly. Skyscraper: The highest sail on a ship-the one closest to the sky-was called a "skyscraper." Today, skyscrapers are very tall buildings in cities. Other nautical terms: Port refers to the left side of the ship. Starboard refers to the ship's right side. The front of a ship is the bow, and the back of the ship it its stern. And to do this we use a spinning wheel. There are several different types-- the one shown here is called a "great", a "wool", or a "walking" wheel. With my left hand I hold the wool off at an angle so that the same little bit that's already been twisted falls off and on the tip of the spindle. This pushes twists up towards my hand. I try to stay ahead of that by stretching out the wool to the thickness I want. My right hand powers the wheel, makes the spindle turn, and my two hands work together so that I get the thickness of yarn that I want. Once the spindle is full, I have to stop and then take the yarn off before I can do more spinning. In Advance: Talk to children about opposites. Encourage them to come up with examples of what they think opposites are. Invite children to play the following movement games to help them learn about opposites. In, Out, Up, Down! Invite the children to make a large circle and teach them this rhyming dance! Take three steps in and clap, clap, clap Take three steps out and snap, snap, snap Bend your body way down low Now push your body and up you go! Step to the right and clap, clap, clap Step to the left and snap, snap, snap Wiggle your body with a chill. Hold yourself steady and stand very still. Use masking tape or chalk to make a large circle on the ground. Reinforce the concept of opposites with a game of Simon says. Substitute a teacher's name for Simon. Simon says: Stand inside the circle. Simon says: Stand outside the circle. Simon says: Shake your hands very fast. Simon says: Move your hands very slowly. Simon says: Loudly yell "hooray!" Simon says: Quietly whisper "hooray!" Simon says: Run in place. Simon says: Stand very still. Simon says: Make a sad face. Simon says: Make a happy face. Problem Solving: Finding Opposites. Read several books to children to familiarize them with the concept of opposites. Title: How to Delete an Album on Facebook Facebook is a popular social media platform that allows users to share photos and memories with friends and family. However, there may come a time when you want to delete an album from your profile. This could be due to various reasons such as wanting to declutter your account or removing sensitive information. Whatever the reason, this tutorial will guide you through the process of deleting an album on Facebook. **Step 1: Log into Your Account** Start by logging into your Facebook account using your email address or phone number and password. It's important to ensure you are logged into the correct account if you manage multiple profiles. **Step 2: Navigate to the Album** Once logged in, find the 'Photos' tab which can usually be found under your cover photo or within the 'Explore' section located on the left side of your News Feed. Click on 'Albums' to view all the albums you have created. Scroll down until you locate the album you wish to delete. **Step 3: Open the Album Settings** Hover over the album thumbnail until a small pop-up menu appears. Click on the three dots ('...') button at the top right corner of this box. A dropdown list will appear; select 'Edit album'. This action takes you to the album editing page where several options like changing the album name, adding contributors, etc., are available. But since we aim to delete the album, proceed to the next step. **Step 4: Initiate Deletion Process** On the bottom right corner of the album editing page, click on the 'Delete Album' link. Please note that this option might slightly vary depending upon recent updates made by Facebook. If you cannot see 'Delete Album', look for a trash bin icon or similar visual cue. After clicking 'Delete Album', another dialog box will appear asking for confirmation. Remember, once confirmed, this action cannot be undone! Therefore, carefully read the warning message before moving forward. **Key Tips:** * Be certain about deleting the album because there's no way back after confirming. * Make sure you really want to delete the entire album. Individual photo deletion is also possible. **Guidelines:** * Always double-check whose access permissions you're revoking while performing these actions. * Consider downloading the photos first if they hold sentimental value or importance. **Step 5: Final Confirmation** If you've decided to go ahead, check the box indicating that you understand that the album and its contents will be permanently deleted. Then press the 'Confirm' button. And voila! You've successfully deleted an album from your Facebook profile. The selected album should now disappear from your Photos > Albums section. Repeat these steps whenever you need to remove any other albums. Remember, managing your online presence responsibly includes regularly reviewing what content remains accessible. Regularly cleaning up old posts and albums ensures privacy compliance and reduces potential security risks associated with outdated data. Okay well let's go pick one out. E Prime (it means English Prime) is a way of speaking English without using the verb "to be" in any way ("be, is, am, are, was, were, been, and being"). Instead, an E Prime speaker or writer uses different verbs like "to become," "to remain," and "to equal" or they might choose to rearrange the sentence to show that the "thing" does not actually "act". For example, in E Prime, a writer would change the statement "Mistakes were made" to "Joe made mistakes." This change in wording reveals an actor (Joe) where the previous form concealed the actor. Users of E Prime would consider the changed sentence more accurate. This is what he gets didn't you ever notice that? No I moved that when I closed the door. Look Bro it's the same page. Move 'er arms so she can cheer. Title: How to Change a Folder Icon on Windows 7 Windows 7 allows you to customize your desktop by changing folder icons. This can be done for personalization or organization purposes. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to do this: ### Step 1: Prepare Your New Icon Before changing a folder icon, you need to have the new icon ready. You can create an .ico file using various online tools or software like IcoFX, or use existing ones from downloaded programs or websites. Make sure that the icon is saved in an accessible location on your computer. **Key Tip**: The icon should be in .ico format as Windows 7 does not natively support other formats for changing folder icons. ### Step 2: Open File Explorer Click on the 'Start' button (the round orb usually located at the bottom left corner of your screen) and then select 'Computer'. This will open the File Explorer window. ### Step 3: Locate the Target Folder Within the File Explorer window, navigate through your directories until you find the folder whose icon you want to change. Once found, right-click on it and choose 'Properties' from the dropdown menu. ### Step 4: Access the Customize Tab In the Properties dialog box, click on the 'Customize' tab which is typically the second tab after the 'General' tab. ### Step 5: Click on 'Change Icon...' Button Under the 'Customize' tab, look for the section named 'Folder Pictures' and within it, there is a button labeled 'Change Icon...'; click on it. ### Step 6: Browse for the New Icon A new dialog box titled 'Change Icon' appears. Here, click on the 'Browse' button to locate your newly prepared .ico file. ### Step 7: Selecting the New Icon After clicking 'Browse', another dialog box called 'Open' shows up. Navigate to where you stored your new icon, select it, and hit 'Open'. ### Step 8: Apply Changes Back in the 'Change Icon' dialog box, you'll now see your selected icon displayed. If satisfied, press 'OK'. ### Step 9: Confirm Final Changes You are returned to the 'Properties' dialog box. Press 'Apply' here to confirm your changes. A preview of your new folder icon should appear instantly. ### Step 10: Close Property Box & Enjoy! Finally, press 'OK' to close the properties dialog box. Congratulations! You've successfully changed your folder icon in Windows 7. **Guideline Note**: Changing folder icons doesn't affect their functionality; they only serve aesthetic purposes. Also remember that these steps must be repeated for every folder you wish to modify since folders revert to their default appearance upon being moved or copied. " We all are impatient and want our computer to work as fast as possible, and certainly faster than the guy's at the next desk! Many different factors determine how fast your computer gets things done. Processor speed is one factor. But what determines the processor's speed? |System clock rate = rate of an electronic pulse used to synchronize processing (Only one action can take place between pulses.) Measured in megahertz (MHz) where 1 MHz = 1 million cycles per second or gigahertz (GHz) where 1 GHz = 1 billion cycles per second. This is what they are talking about if they say a computer is a 2.4 GHz machine. It's clock rate is 2.4 billion cycles per second. Bigger number = faster processing |Bus width = the amount of data the CPU can transmit at a time to main memory and to input and output devices. (Any path bits travel is a bus.) An 8-bit bus moves 8 bits of data at a time. Think of it as "How many passengers (bits) can fit on the bus at once to go from one part of the computer to another." Bigger number = faster transfer of data |Word size = a word is the amount of data the CPU can process at one time. An 8-bit processor can manipulate 8 bits at a time. Bigger the number = faster processing You want a nice match between the word size and the bus size and the clock. It wouldn't do any good to have a bus that can deliver data 128 bits at a time, if the CPU can only use 8 bits at a time and has a slow clock speed. A huge line of data would form, waiting to get off the bus! When computers gets clogged like that, bad things can happen to your data. It's like people waiting to get into the theater. Although boats float, their weight makes them settle a little way into the water. This means they create some resistance or drag when they move through the water. The bow (front) of a boat is V-shaped and curved. This raises the boat up as it goes faster, and helps reduce drag. Boats are powered by sails, oars, or engines that turn propellers at the rear. The propellers push water backwards, and this backward thrust moves the boat forwards. A hydrofoil is a high-speed boat that seems to fly along almost out of the water. The boat has small underwater wings called foils. These work like the aerofoil wings of an aeroplane. As the boat speeds along, the foils generate a force upwards that lifts its hull (body) clear of the water. This reduces drag and helps the hydrofoil go faster. Submarines can float on the sea, sink just beneath the surface, or dive to the seabed. They dive or surface using tanks that work like a fish's swim bladder. When the tanks are filled with water, the submarine dives. When they are filled with air, it surfaces. A submarine can select its level in the sea by changing the mixture of air and water in its tanks. And we brought some school work to do if you don't mind. oh, that's lovely now you can just XXX. Hello young learners! Today, we are going to talk about something called "bases" in mathematics. You might have heard about coordinates before - like when your teacher talks about finding the x and y coordinates on a graph. Well, bases are closely related to coordinates! Let's imagine you have a big box full of different colored balls - let's say red, blue, green, and yellow ones. Now, suppose you want to describe where one particular ball is located inside the box using just two numbers instead of having to give its exact position. How could you do that? One way would be to choose two special balls, which we will call our "base balls." Let's say we pick the red and blue balls as our base balls. To describe the location of another ball, we can ask ourselves: "How many red base balls does it take to reach the ball I am looking for?" and "How many blue base balls do I need to add to get there?" These two questions will give us the first and second numbers, respectively. So now, if we wanted to describe the location of a green ball, maybe we would need three red base balls and no blue base balls. In this case, our description would be "(3, 0)." Easy peasy! Now here comes the twist: what happens if our box also had some purple balls too, which were actually made up of equal parts red and blue mixed together? Wouldn't it make more sense to include those in our description since they're part of our box too? Absolutely! And guess what - that's exactly how mathematicians think about things like these. They allow their base elements to be combinations of other base elements so long as they remain linearly independent. When it comes to fields of complex numbers, or pairs of numbers written as a+bi, the idea remains similar. Just like how we chose red and blue balls earlier, mathematicians often choose {(1, 0)} and {(0, 1)} as their base pair because they behave nicely under addition and multiplication. However, unlike our colorful ball example, these "base vectors" cannot be broken down into smaller pieces, meaning they are already "atomic." Still, sometimes people might wonder whether we could possibly use i=(0,1) itself as one of the base vectors directly. While theoretically possible, doing so doesn't offer any practical advantages over sticking with our good old friends (1,0) and (0,1). I hope this gives you an intuitive understanding of what a basis is all about without getting tangled up in complicated math jargon. Keep exploring, keep questioning, and remember - math is fun! Copyright 2007 Dorling Kindersley A lens is a piece of transparent plastic or glass that can make things seem to change size. It works by bending light rays so they appear to come from a slightly different place. Some lenses make things look nearer and bigger. Others make things look smaller and further away. Without their spectacle lenses, many people would be unable to see clearly, read books, or drive safely. A contact lens is a tiny piece of plastic or glass that rests on the front of the eyeball. It bends light rays before they enter the eye in ways that help the wearer to see more clearly. The two main types of lens are called concave and convex. A concave lens is thin in the middle and thick at the edges, so it seems to "cave" inwards. It makes light rays bend outwards, or diverge. A convex lens works in the opposite way. It is thicker in the middle and thinner at the edges. Light rays passing through a convex lens bend inwards, or converge. A lighthouse must send a long beam of light far out to sea. To do that a very large and heavy lens would normally be needed. Instead, lighthouses use a specially shaped Fresnel lens. It has steps like a staircase, each of which helps to bend the light into a single, powerful beam. A Fresnel lens can be made from glass or lightweight plastic. By shining a powerful beam of light through a transparent photographic slide, a projector can make a much larger image on a wall. The little image on the slide is shined through a concave lens, which spreads the light rays outwards. The further away the projector is from the wall, the bigger the image becomes. To magnify means to increase in apparent size. If you look closely at a magnifying glass, you can see that it is a large convex lens, thicker in the centre than at the edges. When you hold a magnifying glass over an object, it makes light rays from the object seem to come from a nearer point, causing it to look bigger than it really is. The Historic Center of Quito, Ecuador is one of the largest, least-altered and best-preserved historic centers in the Americas. This center was, together with the historic centre of Krak w in Poland, the first to be declared World Heritage Site by UNESCO on 18 September 1978. The Historic Centre of Quito is in the center south of the capital on an area of 320 hectares (790 acres), and is considered one of the most important historic areas in Latin America.There are about 130 monumental buildings (which hosts a variety of pictorial art and sculpture, mostly of religious inspired in a multi-faceted range of schools and styles) and 5,000 properties registered in the municipal inventory of heritage properties. Checking plugins in Google Chrome is an important task that can help you ensure your browser's performance, security, and functionality. This tutorial will provide a step-by-step guide on how to check plugins in Google Chrome, along with detailed explanations, key tips, and guidelines. Step 1: Open Google Chrome First, launch Google Chrome on your computer. If you haven't installed it yet, download and install it from <https://www.google.com/chrome/>. Step 2: Access the Plugin Page Click on the three dots ( ) located at the top right corner of the browser window. From the dropdown menu, hover over "More Tools," then click on "Extensions." Alternatively, you can directly type `chrome://extensions` in the address bar and press Enter. You will now be taken to the Extensions page where all your installed plugins are listed. Key Tip: Regularly checking this page allows you to monitor which plugins are currently active, update them if necessary, or remove any unused ones to maintain optimal browser performance. Step 3: Review Installed Plugins On the Extensions page, you will see a list of all installed plugins. Each plugin has its own card displaying its name, version, author, and enabled status. Here, you can review and manage these extensions as needed. Guideline: Aim to keep only essential plugins activated. Having too many unnecessary plugins may lead to slower browsing speeds, increased memory usage, and potential security risks. Step 4: Update Outdated Plugins If there are updates available for any of your plugins, they will have an update button next to their names. Click on the 'Update' button to get the latest version, ensuring better compatibility, improved features, and enhanced security. Key Tip: Keeping your plugins up-to-date reduces vulnerabilities and ensures smooth functioning within Google Chrome. Step 5: Enable or Disable Plugins To enable or disable a specific plugin, simply toggle the switch found under its name. When enabled, the switch should be blue and switched to the right; when disabled, it should appear gray and switched to the left. Guideline: Only enable plugins when you need them. This approach minimizes resource consumption while maximizing privacy and security. Step 6: Remove Unwanted Plugins Locate any unwanted plugins by scrolling through the list. Once identified, click on the 'Remove' button associated with the plugin. Confirm removal when prompted to do so. Removing unneeded plugins frees up space and resources, enhancing overall browser performance and safety. Step 7: Manage Permissions Some plugins require certain permissions to operate correctly. To view and modify these permissions, click on the 'Details' button beneath each plugin. Carefully assess whether the requested permissions align with what the extension offers and your comfort level regarding data sharing. Limit permissions wherever possible to protect your personal information. In conclusion, regularly checking plugins in Google Chrome provides several benefits such as optimized performance, heightened security, and informed decision making regarding extensions. By following this comprehensive tutorial, users can confidently navigate their way around managing plugins, improving both their online experience and peace of mind. Field Hockey Drills For Kids These field hockey drills for kids will get them in shape and ready to play. The sport of field hockey requires a lot of running, stopping and starting. Shooting to score is a key element. Use these drill to motivate the kids with a bit of fun and play incorporated into learning and working out. - Up and Back. Begin field hockey drills for kids with simple running. Just running back and forth is boring, so instead, play a game of up and back. Line the kids along one end of the field and instruct them to run to the half-field line and back. Then add to the half-field line, back to the beginning and then all the way to the other end. Then add half-field, back, all the way and back to the half-field mark. Finally, go from start to half-field, back, then all the way, back to the half-field, back to the opposite end then a long run all the way back to the start. Make it a race to motivate the players to be competitive and work harder. - Dribble dribble. The next piece in field hockey drills for kids involves practicing dribbling. Now that they can run the length of the field, add a stick and ball to the mix. Line the team up at one end of the field, each with their stick and ball and have them dribble, or move the ball with the stick, repeating the Up and Back drill. - Down the line. Building on the previous field hockey drills for kids, line the kids up at one end of the field each with a stick in hand. Only give a ball, or puck, to the player on the left end of the line. On go, the first player passes to the second player. The second then to the third and down the line. When the last player receives the ball, they then begin passing it back down the line. This skill is difficult as they are running. Have them begin at a jogging pace and build to a run. - Across the field. Now that the team can pass, build on the previous field hockey drills for kids by lining the players diagonally across the field from one end to the other. Have them pass the ball, or puck, up and back. - Shoot and score. Now that they have running and passing down, line them up at the half-field line and give them all a ball. On go, each player, beginning at the left end of the line, shoots the ball at the goal. Repeat until they can all hit the goal. This will improve on their aim. - Run and score. The final piece in field hockey drills for kids is letting them run down the field and shoot for a goal one at a time. This incorporates all the offensive skills at once. Once upon a time, there was a messy horse named Dotty. Dotty liked to play in the mud all day. She would roll and jump in the puddles. Her friends, the birds, would sing and laugh as they watched her play. One day, a little girl named Lily came to the farm. She saw Dotty and said, "Oh, what a messy horse! Can I play with her?" The farmer said, "Yes, you can play with Dotty. But first, let's give her a bath." Lily and the farmer took Dotty to the water. They used soap and a big brush to clean her. Dotty liked the bath and soon she was clean and shiny. Lily said, "Wow, Dotty, you look so pretty now!" Lily and Dotty played all day. They ran and jumped in the grass. The birds sang and laughed as they watched them play. As the sun went down, Lily hugged Dotty and said, "I had so much fun today. I will come back to play with you again." From that day on, Dotty stayed clean and pretty. She would still play in the mud sometimes, but she always took a bath after. And Lily came to visit her messy horse friend, Dotty, every week. They liked to play in the sand and look at the water. Sometimes they saw fish or birds or boats. But today they saw something big and gray and spouty. It was a whale! "Wow, look at that whale!" Tom said. "It is so big and strong and loud!" "I want to see it closer!" Lily said. "Maybe it will talk to us or play with us!" Mom and dad shook their heads. "No, Lily, you can't go near the whale. It is too far and too deep and too dangerous. The whale might not like it if you bother it. It might get angry or scared or hurt you." "But why?" Lily asked. "The whale looks nice and friendly. It is just swimming and splashing and having fun. Maybe it is lonely and wants some friends." Tom decided to agree with Lily. He thought mom and dad were ignorant. They did not know anything about whales. He wanted to learn more about them and make them happy. He said to Lily, "Let's go find the whale. We can sneak away when mom and dad are not looking. Once upon a time there was a little girl. Her name was Lucy. She was three years old and loved to imagine things. One sunny summer day, Lucy was playing in her garden and imagining herself as a princess. She looked up to see a beautiful white carriage pulled by two horses. On top of the carriage was a golden horn. The driver of the carriage smiled at Lucy and said, "Would you like a ride, your Highness?" Lucy was so excited and clapped her hands. She climbed into the carriage and they began their ride around the town. As they drove, Lucy noticed there were many other carriages on the streets. All the carriages were so organized and they looked like they had been waiting for her in lines. At the end of their ride, the driver said to Lucy, "This is your carriage, your Highness. Thank you for joining me!" Lucy thanked him and then looked one more time at the golden horn on the top of the carriage. She waved goodbye as the carriage drove away and she imagined that one day, when she was older, she'd have a carriage of her own. At the end of this lesson the students should be able to add different objects together to find out how many objects there are total 1. The students should be able to explain up to 3 different reasons that being able to add is important 1. 5 colorful building blocks 2. 5 pieces of paper with the numbers 1 through 10 on them The teacher should start out by explaining that addition is simply the combination of 2 or more different groups of numbers. For example if you have a pile with 2 objects in it and another pile with one object in it you get three. The teacher should count the separate piles, then put the piles together and count them again. Next the teacher should get five students to come up to the front of the room. The students should be split up into 2 groups one with 3 individuals and the other with 2. The students in the first group should each get pieces of paper with the numbers 1, 2 and 3 written on them. The students in the second group should get the numbers 4 and 5. The class should then count the students in the first group. The teacher should then say that we are now going to add 2 and 3 together. As the group of two moves to the group of three the teacher should say that we have just added 2 to 3. The students should then count the this new group and come up with the number five. The teacher should not move on if the students do not readily recount the new group and do not seem to understand that the groups have now been combined. The teacher should now talk to the students about the different ways we can use addition in our everyday lives. The teacher should give the example of figuring out how many fish are in the fish tank. The fish could be separated in several ways depending on what fish are in the tank. If there is 1 black fish and 2 gold fish this is an easy number for the children to add. The teacher should then have the students take turns going around the room and looking for things they could add together. The teacher should be accepting of most anything the students want to add. If one student want to add three block and one desk that is ok. The teacher should then ask the students about things in their homes that they can add and ask everybody to think of something at home tonight that it could be beneficial to add. As a conclusion to this lesson the teacher should have several different objects that she has gathered for the students to add. Each student should get a chance to do this. If any students make a mistake the teacher should correct them and help the students by having them count each pile and then count the piles together. The students should be informally evaluated based on how the teacher has observed them answering questions. A short quiz could be used as well, but should not take more then five minutes to finish. Give it here Adam let's see if I can repair it. Growing independence and fluency Rational: To increase reading speed and comprehension, students must learn to read silently. They must learn to read without saying the words out loud. In this lesson students will learn the steps they must take in order to read silently. They will read decodable books of their choice. In this lesson students are encouraged to read quickly and for comprehension. Materials: One poster with The house was quiet until Tom and Jane came home and started to make cookies. They dropped the flour on the floor and broke five eggs. The sink over flowed when they were washing the dishes, but the cookies tasted great. on it, One poster with The popcorn exploded in the microwave. on it, a library of decodable books and copies of a short decodable story for the whole class to read for an assessment. 1. I would begin the lesson by asking the children "why we read?". "We read to learn about things. If we read slowly we forget what the first sentence said and if we all read out loud at the same time; we might get distracted by the others around us." I would have the children all take out their readers and I would have them all read out loud to show them how distracting that could be. "Don't you think that it would be a good idea if the whole class learned to read silently?" 2. " To learn how to be a good silent reader, you must start out slowly. I am going to show you the three steps to reading silently. The first step is to read in a whisper. Watch as I read these sentences in a whisper. 'The house was quiet until Tom and Jane came home and started to make cookies. They dropped the flour on the floor and broke five eggs. The sink over flowed when they were washing the dishes, but the cookies tasted great.' Now I am going to read the story again and this time I am only going to move my lips. Did everyone see my lips move? While I was reading I was saying the words to myself. Now watch as I read the story silently." 3. "When I am reading silently, sometimes I come across words that I don't know. When this happens, I have to take an educated guess. First, I try to blend the phonemes together to get the correct pronunciation. Then I try to read the rest of the sentence and try to figure out what the author is trying to say. This is called cross-checking. We have talked about this before, but I wanted to review it because it is very important in silent reading. Watch as I use cross-checking to figure out this sentence. ' The popcorn expl /o/ ded in the m/i/cr/o/wave.' Wait that did not make sense. I am going to reread that sentence 'The popcorn exploded in the microwave.' Oh! That makes much more sense. I went back and tried to pronounce the phonemes in different ways and then I went back and reread the sentence until it made sense." 4. " Class I would like for you to all go over to the bookshelf and find a book that you have already read or that you would like to read and find a comfortable place to sit. Don't pick a book that is too hard for you and if you are not sure if a book is too hard for you, use the two finger rule. Remember if there are two words that you don't know on a page that the book is probably to hard. I want everyone to read their book either at a whisper or silently, then we are going to tell your group about the book you just read. This is not a contest to see how fast you can read, this is for you to practice your reading comprehension skills." For Assessment: I would call on them to tell me about the book that they read. I could judge the amount of their comprehension by asking general to detailed questions about the book they choose to read. I would also have them read a short paragraph silently, then have them put events in the order that they occurred in the story. While they were reading, I would also pay attention to who was whispering, who was reading by moving their lips and who has begun to really read silently. Reference: My third grade teacher, Mrs. Bettye Hixon St. school, Chattanooga Tennessee 1988. Wilson, P. (1992). Among Nonreaders: Voluntary Reading, Reading Achievment, and the Development of Reading Habits. In C. Temple and P. Collins (Eds.), Stories and readers:New Perspectives on literature in the elementary classroom (pp. 157-169). Norwood, MA: Christopher Gordon. Click here to return to Insights. Should we put this book back in the box? Color blindness is very sensitive to changes in material. A red-green color blind person may be unable to see the difference between colors on a map printed on paper. The same map on a computer screen or television may appear normally. In addition, some color blind people find it easier to tell the difference between colors on artificial materials, such as plastic or in acrylic paints, than on natural materials, such as paper or wood. Thirdly, for some color blind people, color can only be distinguished if there is enough color: thin lines might appear black, but a thicker line of the same color can be seen in the correct color. New Zealand used to have more rare species, but some, mostly birds, were hunted to extinction early in its history. The giant Moa, "Dinornis", is a famous example. The group of birds which the moa belongs to had been in existence since the Cretaceous period. Humans arrived in New Zealand about a thousand years ago, when many moa were living, especially on the South Island. Archaeological sites with evidence of moa hunting are all over New Zealand. The moa became extinct about five hundred years ago. Extinctions closer to today have been caused by habitat change and introduced species like rats, dogs, cats, and possums, which kill the native birds and lizards, or eat their eggs. Sure thing! Let me try my best to explain the concept of motion and forces in a way that's easy for grade-schoolers to understand. Imagine you're playing with a toy car on a smooth floor. When you push the car gently, it moves slowly. But when you give it a harder shove, it goes faster. That's because the car experiences a force (your push), which makes it accelerate and move faster. In our example, we're talking about a particle (which could be anything really - a small ball, a dot moving on a screen, etc.) that has a certain mass (let's say it's like a heavy bowling ball). At different moments in time, this particle feels various pushes or pulls (called "forces") that make it change its speed or direction. In fact, one of the most important rules in physics called Newton's Second Law states that the total force acting on an object equals the product of its mass and acceleration. We often represent this relationship using the formula F = m * a. Now, let's think about finding out the particle's velocity after some time. Velocity tells us not only how fast something is going but also the direction it's heading towards. To figure out the velocity, first, we must determine the acceleration by dividing the force by mass (a = F / m). Then, we integrate the acceleration over time to get the velocity function (v(t)). Finally, plugging in a specific moment (in seconds), we obtain the velocity value at that point. However, I notice there was a concern about writing the final answer in vector form instead of providing just the magnitude. While the question asks for the magnitude specifically, leaving the response in vector form allows others to better visualize the direction along with the speed easily. A vector is simply an arrow pointing somewhere, where the length represents the size while the orientation signifies the direction. So here, instead of giving merely numbers, imagine holding an imaginary arrow starting from where the particle currently sits, stretching it 30 units long horizontally, followed by another stretch upwards making it reach 75 units high - voila, you got yourself a vector representing the velocity! I hope this explanation helps clarify the main idea behind calculating velocities based on applied forces without delving into complex mathematical concepts. Just remember: Force leads to acceleration, integration gets you velocity, and sometimes, vectors are more fun than mere magnitudes! Do you see any pants over here? Here throw this paper away in the garbage. The way the articles are arranged (in alphabet order) in the "Microp dia" and "Macrop dia" is very accurate. Non-English letters are ignored and articles with numbers such as "War of 1812" are arranged as if the number had been written out ("War of Eighteen-twelve"). If the articles have the same names, articles about persons go first, then by places, then by things. People with the same names are arranged first alphabetically by country and then by their time. Similarly, places that have the same names are arranged by alphabet by the country they are in. [still trying to replace the cap on the marker] Bulls can often be fierce and dangerous, especially in the presence of their herd of cows and heifers. In the wild, they will often fight each other over mating rights and their herds of cows and will use their horns to "gore" each other. Some bulls will fight to the death: others will fight until either one of the bulls decides to run off. They also protect the herds from other animals such as wolves, jackals, bears, tigers and lions. On farms, bulls are usually quieter and more docile and can be led by a nose-ring by their owners, but they can be aggressive with other bulls and with strange people or animals who might get too near his herd. Dairy bulls like Jerseys and Holsteins tend to be more aggressive than bulls of beef breeds like Hereford and Angus. Not all cattle have horns. DMing (Direct Messaging) a girl can be an exciting yet nerve-wracking experience. It's important to approach this situation with respect, confidence, and genuine intentions. Here is a comprehensive guide that will help you craft thoughtful messages while keeping the conversation engaging and enjoyable for both parties involved. **Step 1: Choose Your Medium** Determine which platform or app you want to use for direct messaging. Common options include Instagram, Twitter, Facebook Messenger, Snapchat, LinkedIn, or even text message platforms like WhatsApp or Telegram. Make sure your chosen medium aligns with her preferences and where she might be most comfortable responding. *Key tip:* Avoid using dating apps as a means of initiating a casual conversation via DM, unless there has been previous interaction within the app itself. **Step 2: Research Her Interests** Before sending any message, take some time to learn about her interests by looking at her social media profiles. This could involve checking out her favorite music artists, movies, books, hobbies, or recent activities. Knowing these details allows you to tailor your opening line and show genuine interest in getting to know her better. *Guideline*: Be discreet when researching; avoid appearing overly eager or invasive. Remember, privacy matters! **Step 3: Craft a Compelling Opening Line** Avoid generic openers such as "hi" or "hey." Instead, create a unique and engaging opener based on what you learned from Step 2. For example, if she enjoys reading, ask her opinion on a book you recently read or are interested in reading. If she shares pictures of her art projects, compliment her creativity and share something related that inspires you. *Key Tip:* Keep your initial message short but intriguing - aim for around 50-70 characters. Longer messages may feel overwhelming initially, making it harder for her to respond. **Step 4: Show Genuine Interest** Once you have broken the ice, continue asking questions related to her interests and experiences. Listen carefully to her responses and build upon them naturally during the conversation. By doing so, you demonstrate sincere curiosity and investment in knowing more about her. *Guidelines:* a. Use open-ended questions to encourage longer answers and further discussion. b. Respond appropriately and actively engage in the dialogue without dominating the conversation. c. Share personal stories and insights relevant to the topic being discussed. **Step 5: Maintain Appropriate Boundaries** While building rapport, ensure that your conversations remain friendly, lighthearted, and respectful. Refrain from discussing sensitive topics too early in the relationship, such as politics, religion, or past relationships. Additionally, avoid sharing excessive information about yourself all at once. *Key Tips:* a. Stay mindful of cultural differences and potential misunderstandings due to language barriers. b. Never pressure her into disclosing private details or divulging intimate information. **Step 6: Observe Her Reactions & Adapt Accordingly** Pay close attention to her tone, response speed, and level of engagement throughout the conversation. These factors can provide valuable insight into whether she is enjoying the exchange or feeling uncomfortable. Based on her reactions, adjust your conversational approach accordingly. *Guideline:* Always prioritize her comfort above any desire to steer the conversation towards romantic territory. By following these steps and maintaining a considerate attitude, you increase the likelihood of having meaningful interactions through DMs while fostering mutual respect and understanding between you and the girl you are communicating with. Title: How to Diagnose and Fix a Cylinder 3 Misfire Introduction: A cylinder 3 misfire can be a frustrating issue for car owners as it affects engine performance and fuel efficiency. This problem occurs when there is an improper combustion or ignition failure in cylinder 3 of your vehicle's engine. The following steps will guide you through diagnosing and fixing this common automotive issue. Please note that while some steps are simple enough for beginners, others may require professional assistance. Materials Needed: 1. OBD-II scanner 2. Spark plug socket and ratchet set 3. New spark plug (compatible with your vehicle) 4. Fuel injector cleaner 5. Ignition coil tester 6. Multimeter 7. Basic hand tools (screwdrivers, pliers, etc.) 8. Title: How to Get a WiFi Android Phone With No Contract Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of obtaining an Android phone that operates solely on WiFi, without being tied down by any contractual obligations from mobile carriers. This can be particularly useful if you have consistent access to WiFi networks and want to save costs associated with traditional cellular plans. We'll cover topics such as purchasing a suitable device, setting it up, and understanding its limitations compared to regular smartphones. Step 1: Choose Your Device The first step is selecting the right WiFi-only Android phone. While many newer models do offer this feature, they might still include carrier branding or preinstalled apps which could affect performance. It's advisable to look into devices specifically designed for WiFi usage - often marketed as 'WiFi tablets'. These tend to provide a pure Android experience free from carrier interference. Examples include Samsung Galaxy Tab A7 Lite, Amazon Fire HD 10 etc. Always check product specifications before buying. Key Tip: Make sure the chosen device supports all the features (like Google Play Store) you need, even though there's no cellular connectivity involved. Step 2: Purchase the Device Once you've decided on your preferred model, proceed to purchase it online or at a physical store. Be aware of potential shipping delays due to global circumstances. If buying in-store, consider checking multiple retailers for competitive pricing and availability. Guideline: Stick to reputable sellers to avoid scams or receiving faulty products. Step 3: Unbox the Device After receiving your new gadget, carefully unpack everything. Inside should be the device itself, charging adapter, USB cable, possibly earbuds, and some documentation like user manuals and warranty information. Step 4: Charge the Device Plug one end of the provided USB cable into the charger and the other end into your new phone. Wait until the battery reaches full charge; this usually takes several hours depending on the battery size and capacity. Step 5: Power On & Connect to WiFi Press and hold the power button until the screen lights up. Follow the prompts to select language and region settings. Then, navigate to Settings > Network & Internet > WiFi. Select your home network from the list of available connections, enter the password when prompted, then tap 'Connect'. Key Tip: Remember, only WiFi calling and messaging services will work on a WiFi-only device since it lacks cellular capabilities. Step 6: Set Up Google Account If this is your first time using an Android device, you'll likely need to create or sign into a Google account during setup. Having a Google account allows you to download apps from the Google Play Store, sync data across different devices, back up important files, and more. Step 7: Download Necessary Apps Visit the Google Play Store and download necessary applications based on your needs, e.g., social media platforms, productivity tools, entertainment apps, etc. Keep in mind that certain apps may require additional permissions due to their functionalities. Step 8: Familiarize Yourself with the Interface Spend some time navigating around your new device to familiarize yourself with where things are located within the operating system. Customizing elements like wallpaper, ringtones, font sizes, etc., can make the transition smoother. Conclusion: By following these steps, you now own a WiFi Android phone free from contracts! Enjoy exploring its functionality while connected to trusted WiFi networks. Just remember its limitations regarding voice calls and SMS outside of supported WiFi environments. Elsewhere, chopsticks are often used in restaurants serving oriental cuisines. the layered frameworks aid rapid development of applications by providing existing code for common tasks. Apple provides its own software development tools, most prominently an integrated development environment called Xcode. Xcode provides interfaces to compilers that support several programming languages including C, C++, Objective-C, and Swift. For the Apple-Intel transition, it was modified so that developers could build their applications as a universal binary, which provides compatibility with both the Intel-based and PowerPC-based Macintosh lines. First and third-party applications can be controlled programatically using the AppleScript framework, retained from the classic Mac OS, or using the newer Automator application that offers pre-written tasks that do not require programming knowledge. They thanked Ben for saving them. They said they were sorry for being foolish and reckless. They said they learned their lesson. They said they would never go in the chamber again. [Chi shakes head no to MOT's question] [repeatedly pulls the top silver man off the bottom man, and then puts them back together] Estimating distances using your thumb and eyes is a handy skill that can come in useful in many situations, such as hiking, sports, or even driving. This technique, also known as "thumbing," allows you to quickly gauge the distance between yourself and an object without needing any specialized equipment. Here's our comprehensive guide on estimating distances by using your thumb and eyes: ### Step 1: Understand the concept The basic idea behind this method is comparing the size of an object with the width of your outstretched thumb when held at arm's length. By knowing the actual size of the object or having a reference point, you can estimate its distance based on the angular size subtended by your thumb. The further away the object, the smaller its angular size will be relative to your thumb. ### Step 2: Familiarize yourself with angular size Angular size refers to the angle formed by two lines connecting the outer edges of an object and your eyes. It determines how large an object appears from your perspective. For example, if you hold a coin at arm's length, its angular size will be much larger than when you hold it twice as far away because the angle it forms with your eyes decreases as the distance increases. ### Step 3: Measure the size of your thumb To get consistent results, measure the width of your outstretched thumb at arm's length in degrees. Stand about 6 meters (20 feet) away from a wall and mark a spot at eye level. Extend your arm fully and align your thumb with the marked spot. Now, close one eye and notice where your thumb covers the vertical edge of the wall. Use a protractor to measure the angle subtended by your thumb; typically, it should be around 1-2 degrees. Remember this value for future comparisons. ### Step 4: Observe the target object Identify the object whose distance you want to estimate. Ideally, choose an object with a known size, like a tree, car, or building, so you can verify your estimation later. If you don't know the exact size, try to find something similar nearby that has a known dimension. ### Step 5: Hold up your thumb Extend your arm fully and position your thumb so that it obscures part of the target object. Make sure your thumb is aligned vertically with the top or bottom edge of the object for accuracy. You may need to adjust the distance between your eye and the object until your thumb fits nicely over a distinguishable feature, like the roofline of a house. ### Step 6: Compare the sizes Compare the apparent size of the object covered by your thumb with the actual size of your thumb measured earlier. Since both objects now occupy the same angle in your field of view, their ratio corresponds to the inverse ratio of their respective distances. Therefore, if your thumb measures 1 degree and covers half of the target object, the object must be twice as far away as your thumb was during measurement. ### Key Tips & Guidelines * Practice regularly to improve your skills and consistency. * Be aware of potential sources of error, such as parallax due to misalignment between your thumb and the target object. * Consider factors affecting visibility, like atmospheric conditions and lighting. These could influence your perception of the object's size and might require adjustments to your estimates. * Keep in mind that this method provides rough approximations rather than precise measurements. However, it remains a valuable tool for quick assessments in various scenarios. want to tell him that you've read his book? Growing Independence & Fluency Rationale: In order for children to be able to have better comprehension, they must first become more fluent readers. Without learning all the skills needed for decoding, comprehension can become very difficult. Children need to perform repeated readings of the same text in order to gain the characteristics of a successful reader. Some of those characteristics are the ability to read fast, smooth, and with expression. When children become fluent readers, they increase their comprehension which is the ultimate goal of reading! - White Board - Scardey Mouse by Alan MacDonald (enough for everyone including the teacher) How many words in a minute--one minute read? How many words correct (accuracy) Words they are struggling with? 1. Introduce the lesson: "Fluent reading is being able to read a text fast, smooth, and with expression. It is an important part of becoming a successful reader. It takes a lot of practice. Can anyone tell me what it means to read with expression?" (Review the terms making sure everyone knows what each term means before moving on. An example answer would be: "It means to read like you really are the characters and to read happy.") "Today class, we are going to practice rereading the same text or sentence so that we can learn to develop fluency. Let's get started!" (Remind children that sometimes fluent readers do not know every word). 2. Write a practice sentence on the board: (I am very scared of mice!) Read the sentence very slow to the children. For example, I am verrry scaaarred of miiicce! Sound them out slowly again and practice the silent cover-up method. Read the sentence again smoothly and using expression. "Which way did you like it better, slow or fast? Why did you like it better?" (Hopefully the children will say it sounds better fast because you can understand it better). 3. Write another sentence on the board. (There once was a mouse living in my kitchen!) This time, divide the class into groups or partners. Have them practice reading it to each other several times. "Practice smooth reading with expression class." When they have practiced it many times, ask them which way sounded better to them. "This is a great way to practice rereading and becoming a more fluent reader!" 4. Read the book, Scaredy Mouse by Alan MacDonald out loud to the class. "Now, everyone needs to follow along with me in the copy I gave you so we can all learn and become familiar with the text." (READ THE TEXT) "Did everyone see how I read smooth, fast, and used expression? I hope you did because you get to practice it now." 5. "Now I want everyone to read the copy of Scaredy Mouse to themselves. When you're finished, you may choose another book from our class library to practice reading fluently. Remember, if there are more than two words on a page that you don't know, you might need to choose another book." 6. Allow the children at least 30 minutes to accomplish these goals. Call on each child to bring their book to your desk and have them read at least one page from their book out loud to you. Have a checklist ready so you can record their smoothness, speed, and fluency. MacDonald, Alan. Scaredy Mouse, Scholastic Inc., 2002. Thomas, Gina. Quick as a Mouse. Bradley, Alison. The word "schizophrenia" comes from two Greek words that mean "to split" and "mind," because there is a 'split' between what's going on in the person's mind and what is actually happening. A person with schizophrenia does not change between different personalities: they have only one. The condition in which a person has more than one personality, meaning they act like a different person at different times, is dissociative identity disorder. There are no medical tests that can be used to say if a person has schizophrenia or not, so getting a diagnosis depends on which list of symptoms are used. It also depends on the doctor or psychologist who talks to the person. The lists of symptoms include wording like "Disorganized [not organized, or oddly organized] speech present for a significant portion of time". It is difficult to agree on what exactly "disorganized speech" is and how disorganized it has to be. It is also difficult to agree on how long a "significant portion of time" is. Because of this, two doctors or psychologists trying to make a diagnosis may often disagree. One will say that the person is schizophrenic and the other will say he or she is not. Basic components common to most bikes include a seat, pedals, gearing, handlebar, wheels, and brakes, all mounted on a frame. The majority also have a gear shifter. The cyclist's feet push the pedals to make them go around in circles, which moves the chain, which turns the back wheel of the bike to make the bike move forwards. The front wheel is connected to the handlebar, so turning the handlebar from side to side swivels the front wheel which steers the bike. Once there was a little girl called Mandy. She had a wonderful model plane. Every day after school, she would take it out and throw it high into the sky. She loved to watch it soar up high, faster and faster. One day, Mandy was practising throwing her model plane, when suddenly she saw an eagle soaring in the sky. It was so beautiful, she decided she wanted to throw her plane higher so she could be like the eagle. But this time, her plane flew too high and too far away. Mandy began to worry. She searched everywhere, but the plane was gone. She was sad and upset. Suddenly, she heard a rustling sound in the nearby bushes. She looked over and saw her plane was safely perched on a branch. She was so relieved and quickly ran over to retrieve it. From then on, Mandy was very careful when throwing her model plane. She wanted to make sure it was safe and she never wanted to lose it again. An early learning skill your child needs for school success. - As you read a story discuss any words that may be unfamiliar to your child. It is how living things change over time and how new species develop. The theory of evolution explains how evolution works and how living and extinct things have come to be the way they are. The theory of evolution is a very important idea in biology. Theodosius Dobzhansky, a well-known evolutionary biologist, has said: "Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution". Kim Jong-nam (May 10, 1971 - February 13, 2017) was the eldest son of Kim Jong-il, the former leader of North Korea. The first major gold rush in British Columbia started in 1858, after gold was discovered, on the upper reaches of the Fraser River. The sudden onslaught of uncouth Americans led to the declaration that British Columbia shall be a crown colony, instead of the unincorporated region it was, after the Oregon spat. James Douglas was appointed the governor of the newly formed colony, as well as the Colony of Vancouver Island, and for military convenience, the Capital of British Columbia was declared Queensboro, then renamed New Westminster. As massive numbers of Americans came, fears that the Americans would try to bluster themselves another chunk of territory became more and more concrete. Thus, a contingent of Royal Engineers and Marines were deployed to New Westminster. To this day, the neighbourhood of Sapperton, is known, for its history as a site of the barracks for the Engineers, nicknamed the Sappers. The infrastructure ordered by James Douglas, to support the vast amount of capital flowing from the gold industry, caused high debt for British Columbia. By 1864, British Columbia was 200,000 in debt, plus an additional 18,000, from various spats with natives, and constant policing required to maintain law and order. Its vast amount of gold was useless, while BC was in debt. British Columbia amalgamated with Vancouver Island, forming one super-colony. Vancouver Island would undertake the debt, and British Columbia's capital would be in Victoria. The first serious exploration of the Antarctic land was the Nimrod Expedition led by Ernest Shackleton in 1907-09. They were the first to climb Mount Erebus and to reach the South Magnetic Pole. Shackleton himself and three other members of his expedition made several firsts in December 1908 - February 1909. They were the first humans to cross the Ross Ice Shelf, and the Transantarctic Mountain Range (via the Beardmore Glacier). They were the first to set foot on the South Polar Plateau. Jamal says he is sorry about fighting and promises not to do it again. Then Grandpa Joe explains that when he was a young man living in Montgomery, Alabama, African Americans had to ride in the back of city buses. One day in 1955, an African American woman, Rosa Parks, sat in the front of the bus and refused to move. She was arrested. Leaders like Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., helped African Americans in Montgomery to organize and stop riding the buses. Finally, the law was changed. Think About the Selection - Why is Jamal's grandfather so unhappy that Jamal fought to sit at the back of the bus? - What does Jamal learn from talking with his grandfather? How is he different after the talk? - How does Jamal use what he learns from his grandfather in his skit? - Jamal says that he did something "really stupid that turned out to be stupendous instead." CHAPTER III "The Washington Post" is the oldest and most-read daily newspaper in Washington, and it has developed into one of the most reputable daily newspapers in the U.S. It is most notable for exposing the Watergate scandal, among other achievements. The Washington Post Company has multiple media holdings, including a daily free newspaper called the "Express", the Washington Post News Service with Bloomberg News, "Fashion Washington", "El Tiempo Latino" (a Spanish-language publication), the Slate Group, "The Daily Herald" (in Washington state), as well as the education company Kaplan, Inc. "The Washington Post" emphasizes national and political news coverage but also covers regional and local stories. Headquartered in downtown Washington, the newspaper employs journalists at 11 regional bureaus in Maryland and Virginia and 14 international bureaus. Content is shared across titles within the Washington Post Company, and the "Express", in particular, often reprints content from the Associated Press, Getty Images, and other wire sources. Welcome to our exploration of using equations and symbols like a scientist! Today we will learn about how to write mathematical expressions in a fun and easy way. We won't dive into complex college-level topics just yet; instead, let's start with something familiar - making things bigger or smaller! Imagine you wanted to describe growing a plant where its height increases each day according to this rule: Height = Previous Day's Height + 2 inches. In regular writing, expressing this could take up many lines and might confuse your friends. However, by using some special notation called "LaTeX", you can show this idea clearly in one line using symbols. It would look something like this: Height$_{current~day}$ = Height$_{previous~day}$ + 2 ~inches Now imagine having an imaginary friend who always follows the rules of numbers - let's call them Numby. When you tell Numby "Height$_{current~day}$," they know exactly which height you mean because you told them earlier! This concept helps us avoid repetition and makes our ideas more organized and neat. There might come a time when your amazing plant grows taller than your head, reaching great heights beyond what fits on one page! No worries - LaTeX has got your back again. Sometimes, long equations need extra space, but breaking them apart manually isn't fun. With LaTeX, simply place your expression within the "equation*" environment, and it magically takes care of splitting the equation between two pages for you. How cool is that? Lastly, sharing these fantastic new plant-growing equations with others becomes even easier through copy-pasting, dragging-and-dropping, or adding them directly onto toolbars for quick access. Just think about being able to share your work with classmates instantly without any messy cross-outs or erasures. So remember, next time you find yourself describing a multi-step process involving numbers, try thinking of ways to condense it using LaTeX-style notation. Not only does it save time and paper, but it also trains your brain to become more accustomed to handling complex problems step-by-step. Happy equation crafting! Title: How to Build a Simple Steam Engine Introduction: Building a steam engine is an exciting project that allows you to understand the principles behind this essential invention of the Industrial Revolution. This guide will walk you through building a simple model steam engine using easy-to-find materials. Safety first! Be cautious when working with sharp objects and hot surfaces during this process. Tools and Materials Needed: 1. Steel or brass tubing (3/8" outer diameter, 1/4" inner diameter, 2 feet length) - serves as the cylinder 2. Copper pipe (1/4" outer diameter, 3 inches length) - acts as the boiler 3. Aluminum sheet (0.03" thickness, 6 x 6 inch square) - used for making the piston and base plate 4. Plastic straw (7 mm diameter) - functions as the delivery pipe 5. Neodymium magnet (5mm diameter, 3mm height) 6. Small bolt and nut set (5mm diameter, 10mm length) 7. Two ball bearings (5mm internal diameter, 8mm external diameter, 3mm width) 8. Model steam engine valve kit 9. Epoxy glue 10. Drill bits (1/16", 3/32", 1/4", and 5/16") 11. Tap and die set (for tapping threads into the aluminum sheet) 12. Hacksaw or metal cutting saw 13. Bench vise 14. Files and sandpaper 15. Pliers and wrenches 16. Soldering iron and solder 17. Hot glue gun Step 1: Create the Cylinder Block a. Cut two pieces of steel or brass tubing, each 4 inches long, using a hacksaw or metal cutting saw. Make sure both ends are perpendicular to the tube's axis. File any burrs from the cut edges. b. Place one piece vertically in a bench vise, ensuring it remains centered. Using a 1/4" drill bit, bore a hole through its center. Repeat for the second piece. These form the cylinder block. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Keep your cuts clean by marking the tubes before cutting. Use cutting oil if possible to prolong tool life. - Securely clamp the workpiece while drilling; otherwise, it may spin causing injury or ruining the part. Step 2: Prepare the Piston a. Draw a circle with a 1-1/8" radius on the aluminum sheet. Divide the circumference into four equal parts and label them A, B, C, D. b. At points A and C, drill a 1/4" hole through the sheet along the radial lines. Enlarge these holes to 5/1 "My situation was, of course, discovered, and she declared that I should not stay another night under the same roof with an honest family. "Behold me then in the street, utterly destitute! Whither could I creep for shelter? To my father's roof I had no claim, when not pursued by shame-now I shrunk back as from death, from my mother's cruel reproaches, my father's execrations. In North Korea, only Hangeul (Known as Choseongeul in North Korea) is used by law. In South Korea, only Hangeul should be used in most public areas like education, but the other system, Hanja, is still used in some newspapers and professional areas. Hanja is the system of Chinese characters that are used in Korean. It was the only way to write Korean before the creation of Hangeul in the 15th century, and it was common in novels before the 19th century. Here are some other fun activities: Draw songs. Have your child close her eyes and listen to the beginning of a song. Then have her open them and draw whatever the song reminds her of. Copy each other. Hum part of a song your child knows, or play it on an instrument. Then have your child copy you by humming the song himself. You can copy something he hums, too. Change songs. Help your child make up new words to a familiar tune, such as "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star." They don't have to rhyme or even make sense. Adjust the volume. Sing a favorite song, but sing some parts more loudly or softly than usual. You might start a song loudly and fade off at the end, or do the opposite. Another option: Change your volume to match the meaning of words or phrases in the song. Make up songs. Decide to sing instead of talk for part of the day. Don't worry about how you sound. Just have fun singing everything you would normally say, such as "We're having cereal for breakfast." He climbed the tree and grabbed a green ball. He threw it to Lila. She caught it and looked at it. It was hard and bumpy. "What is this?" Lila asked. "I don't know. Maybe it's a toy," Max said. They tried to roll it, bounce it, and kick it. But it was not fun. It was too heavy and dull. "Maybe we can open it," Lila said. She found a rock and hit the green ball with it. It cracked and showed a soft green inside and a big brown seed. "Wow, look at that!" Max said. He climbed down and joined Lila. They touched the green inside. It was creamy and smooth. "Can we eat it?" Lila asked. "I don't know. Maybe we should ask someone," Max said. They saw a lady walking by with a dog. They ran to her and showed her the green ball. "Excuse me, miss. What is this?" Max asked. The lady smiled and said, "That's an avocado. It's a fruit. You can eat it. It's very good for you. And very popular too. Many people like it." "Really? Can we try it?" Lila asked. "Sure, why not. But you have to share it. And don't eat the seed. It's too hard," the lady said. She gave them a spoon and helped them scoop out the green inside. They tasted it. It was mild and buttery. "Mmm, this is yummy," Lila said. "I like it too," Max said. They ate the avocado and thanked the lady. They felt happy and full. They decided to keep the seed and plant it in the park. Maybe they could grow more avocados and share them with their friends. They settled on a spot near the tree and dug a hole. They put the seed in the hole and covered it with dirt. They watered it with their water bottles. "Maybe one day, we'll have our own avocado tree," Lila said. "That would be awesome," Max said. Segar and was inspired by a man Segar knew in Chester, Illinois: Frank "Rocky" Fiegel. Popeye would make his debut in the January 17, 1929 "Thimble Theatre" strip "Dice Island" as a rough sailor for hire. While originally introduced as a minor character (with the actual star of the strips then being Castor Oyl), Popeye's popularity eventually grew to the point where he became the main character of "Thimble Theatre", with the strip now focusing on his adventures and hijinks. Initially, Popeye was portrayed as a very strong and seemingly uneducated but very savvy sailor who would gain extraordinary luck and resilience when he rubbed the magical Whiffle Hen Bernice, but by 1932, he began to favor spinach, crediting it as a healthy source of strength. As the strips started focusing more on Popeye, he began to form a relationship with Castor's sister Olive Oyl after she had left her previous boyfriend Ham Gravy, and the two would eventually go on to become one of the most recognizable couples in cartoon history. The things you need that the doctor uses. Tallahassee was created as the capital of Florida during the territory's second legislative session. It was chosen as the midpoint between St. Augustine and Pensacola, which had been the capitals of the Spanish and British colonies of East Florida and West Florida, respectively. (At that time, very few people lived in the swamps of South Florida.) The first session of Florida's Legislative Council-as a territory of the United States-met on July 22, 1822 at Pensacola and members from St. Augustine traveled fifty-nine days by water to attend. The second session was in St. Augustine and required western delegates to travel perilously around the peninsula on a twenty-eight day trek. During this session, it was decided that future meetings should be held at a half-way point to reduce the distance. Two appointed commissioners selected Tallahassee, at that point an abandoned Apalachee settlement, as a halfway point. In 1824, the third legislative session met there in a crude log capitol. Also in 1824, Marquis de Lafayette, French hero of the American Revolution, returned for a grand tour of the United States. The US Congress voted to give him $200,000 (the same amount he had given the colonies in 1778), US citizenship, and the Lafayette Land Grant, of land that today includes large portions of Tallahassee. In 1845, a Greek revival masonry structure was erected as the Capitol building in time for statehood. I want' 'a look at my book... If human skin touches fire, the skin may burn, which can take some time to heal and to remove the scar. If a fire gives off a large amount of smoke, a person's mouth should be covered with a wet cloth, since people can faint when they breathe in too much smoke. Even a spark, such as from a cigarette, should be kept away from any fuels that very easily catch and spread fire, like gasoline, or cause an explosion, like gunpowder. Be a Scent Detective Fill plastic film canisters with a cotton ball soaked with a strong liquid scent (vanilla, almond, peppermint, vinegar, etc.) Number the canisters, and make a list of which scent is in which canister, so you won't forget which is which. Talk with the students about the sense of smell and how it helps us. Pass around one canister at a time until each child has a canister. Ask them to guess what their smells are.
