Creating a Picture Exchange Communication System (PECS) chart is an effective way to help individuals with communication difficulties express their needs, wants, or feelings through visual cues. This system has been widely used to support children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD), language delays, or other special needs. Here's a comprehensive guide on creating a PECS chart: **Step 1: Assess Needs and Goals** Before starting the process, identify what specific needs you want to address with the PECS chart. Consider whether it will be used for requesting items, sharing emotions, or building social interactions. By understanding your goals, you can tailor the content and layout accordingly. _Key Tip:_ Consult professionals such as speech-language pathologists or behavior analysts who have experience in implementing AAC systems like PECS. They can provide valuable insights into designing an efficient and user-friendly chart based on individual requirements. **Step 2: Select Relevant Pictures** Choose high-quality images that clearly depict the objects, actions, or feelings you wish to include in the chart. Use simple, uncluttered pictures with distinct features so they are easy to recognize even for beginners. You may opt for photographs, clipart, or symbol-based drawings depending upon the user's preferences and abilities. _Guideline:_ Make sure all chosen pictures align with the objectives determined in Step 1. For instance, if one goal is helping the child ask for breaks during activities, incorporate images related to 'break,' 'stop,' or 'rest.' **Step 3: Organize the Layout** Structure the layout by dividing the chart into sections based on categories. Common categories include food & drink, toys, daily activities, and feelings. Arrange these sections logically, either horizontally or vertically, making sure there's enough space between each picture for ease of handling. _Key Tip:_ Prioritize frequently used items or situations near the top left corner since this area tends to attract attention first due to reading habits. **Step 4: Mount Pictures onto Heavy-duty Cards** Print out the selected pictures and mount them onto heavy-duty cards using adhesive backing or lamination. This ensures durability and prevents wear-and-tear over time. Also, consider adding Velcro strips or hooks to both the backside of the photo cards and the corresponding areas on the chart. This feature allows flexibility in updating or changing the content without having to remake the entire chart. _Guideline:_ Keep extra copies of each picture card handy for future replacements or additions. **Step 5: Introduce the PECS Chart** Gradually introduce the PECS chart to the user by initially modeling its usage. Demonstrate how to remove a picture from the chart and exchange it for the actual item or activity. Gradually fade out your assistance until the individual becomes comfortable using the chart independently. _Key Tip:_ Reinforce correct usage with positive feedback, praise, or rewards. Consistently encourage the use of the PECS chart across different settings and people to promote generalization of skills. By following these steps diligently, you can create an effective PECS chart that supports non-verbal communication and fosters independence among users. Remember, patience and consistency are crucial throughout the implementation process. Did you stick it with a pencil? And he Wei wasn't a little caterpillar anymore. Be Distracted for a While: A Comprehensive Guide =============================================== Introduction ------------ In today's fast-paced world, finding time to relax and take a break from daily stresses is essential. This comprehensive guide will teach you how to be distracted for a while, providing much-needed relief from your hectic life. By following these steps, you can learn to momentarily disconnect from responsibilities and immerse yourself in enjoyable activities that promote mental wellbeing. ### Step 1: Identify Your Interests Before seeking distraction, identify what truly interests you. Consider hobbies or pastimes that make you forget about time and bring joy to your life. Examples include reading, painting, playing an instrument, gardening, cooking, or practicing yoga. Engaging in something you enjoy will help shift your focus away from everyday worries and towards personal fulfillment. Key tip: Choose an activity that requires some level of engagement, as passive entertainment (such as watching TV) may not provide the same level of satisfaction or relaxation. ### Step 2: Schedule Time for Distractions Treat being distracted like any other appointment by scheduling it into your day. Set aside at least 30 minutes to engage in your chosen interest. Consistently dedicating this time allows for regular breaks and promotes better overall mental health. Guideline: Make sure to schedule your distraction during a low-stress period. Attempting to find solace amidst peak productivity times might cause additional anxiety. ### Step 3: Eliminate Potential Distractions To fully embrace your designated distraction time, minimize external interruptions. Turn off electronic devices, inform family members or housemates of your plans, and create a quiet, comfortable space where you can concentrate on your chosen activity. Key tip: If possible, select a location separate from areas associated with work or responsibility, such as a home office or living room. Physically removing yourself from these spaces encourages mental separation between leisure and obligation. ### Step 4: Immerse Yourself in the Experience Once you have eliminated potential disturbances, allow yourself to become absorbed in the experience. Focus on the sensory aspects of your chosen activity - observe colors, textures, scents, tastes, and sounds. Give yourself permission to let go of concerns and appreciate the present moment. Guideline: Practice mindfulness techniques, which involve focusing on your breath and observing thoughts without judgment. Mindfulness enhances awareness and fosters greater appreciation for simple pleasures. ### Step 5: Reflect on the Experience After engaging in your distracting activity, spend a few moments reflecting on the experience. Notice how you feel physically, emotionally, and mentally. Recognize if there are noticeable differences in mood or energy levels compared to before beginning your distraction. Key tip: Keep track of your experiences using a journal or diary, noting specific emotions, physical sensations, and insights gained throughout the process. Over time, reviewing these entries can reveal patterns related to stress management and self-care practices. Conclusion ---------- By incorporating deliberate distractions into your routine, you prioritize mental health and enhance overall wellbeing. Remember that taking care of oneself should never be neglected, especially when faced with mounting pressures and expectations. So give yourself permission to be distracted for a while, reaping the benefits of restored balance and renewed vigor. oh, now you want to stand up, huh? If you want to kiss her you better do it now. Solar-Powered Car Project Shared by: fta11207 Solar-Powered Car Project Test Grade Objective: To design and build a solar-powered car that will complete a race in the shortest possible time. The race track will be approximately 10-15 meters. Materials: 1. The vehicle must be your own design. No kits may be used. (Exception: you may use kit parts for the transmission and wheels.) 2. The solar panel and motor must not be glued or messed up. It will be returned to teacher. Find alternate ways to attach them. 3. You may not modify the solar panel or motor in any way. 4. You may use one normal (no super-charged ones) 1.5 volt battery (AA, AAA, C or D) Vehicle specifications: 1. The vehicle must be safe for contestants and spectators (no sharp edges, projectiles, etc). 2. Sunlight will be one power source for the vehicle (and battery if you choose) 3. You may also set up a circuit and use 1 AA battery. 3. An energy-enhancing devices (like mirrors) must be attached to the vehicle. 4. The car must have a chassis that is 3-dimensional. You will NOT be allowed to bolt the axles and wheels directly to the solar module. The solar module cannot be used as the chassis of the car. Race: 1. At race time, vehicles will be placed behind the starting line holding up the car. 2. At the start of the race, the teams will release the vehicle. 4. No push starts. 5. The winner of the race will be the first vehicle to cross the finish line or the farthest car down the lane. 6. One member of each team must wait at the finish line to catch the vehicle. 7. Team members may not accompany or touch the vehicle while it is racing on the track. Vehicles stalled on the track may be retrieved and brought back to the start. You may not touch them on the track once you let them go. If you touch it, it has to come all the way back to the start line to be released again. Grading: The car must be completed on time to race. If you don't race, then you miss those points and or go to the end of the line. This is a Test Grade! Total Points Possible: 90 SEE RUBRIK for specifics on grading! Date: 19 Jan 1995 13:13:05 -0500 From: Margaret Gallegos Subject: Question What is the Pythagorean Theorem? Date: 19 Jan 1995 14:09:42 -0500 From: Dr. Math Margaret Patterson Subject: Re: Question Hi there. Thanks for writing to us. The Pythagorean Theorem has to do with the lengths of the sides of a right triangle. A right triangle is any triangle which has one right angle (an angle of 90 degrees) - like this: |__ If the sides next to the right angle are of lengths a and b, and the third side is of length c, then (a*a) + (b*b) = (c*c). When people say this, they say, "a squared plus b squared equals c squared because when you multiply a number by itself like a*a, you call it a squared. This can be written as a^2. |\ | \ 2 2 2 a | \ c a + b = c | \ |____\ b Some numbers that work in this equation are, 3,4, and 5, and 5, 12, and 13 So if you are told that you have a right triangle whose sides are 3 and 4, like this: |\ | \ Then you can use this theorem to find out what the 3 | \ c third side is. | \ 3*3 + 4*4 = 9 + 16 = 25 = 5*5, so c=5 |____\ 4 Let us know if you have any other questions. -Margaret, Doctor on call. Title: How to Dress Up for a School Concert School concerts are special events where students showcase their talents in music, dance, or drama. It is important to dress appropriately to look presentable and feel confident while performing on stage. This article will guide you through the process of dressing up for a school concert with clear instructions, helpful tips, and useful guidelines. **Step 1: Understand the Dress Code** The first step is understanding what type of attire is expected from you during the performance. Check with your teacher or the event organizer if there's a specific dress code. Some schools may require uniforms, formal wear, cultural costumes, or casual clothing depending on the theme of the concert. Make sure to adhere to these rules as they ensure consistency among performers and respect towards the occasion. **Step 2: Choose Your Outfit** Once you know the dress code, start selecting an outfit that matches the requirements. Here are some suggestions based on common types of dress codes: - Uniforms: If your school requires uniforms, make sure it's clean, ironed, and complete with all accessories such as ties, belts, etc. - Formal Wear: Opt for elegant yet comfortable pieces like tailored trousers or skirts paired with blouses/shirts and sweaters/blazers. Avoid overly flashy or revealing clothes. - Cultural Costumes: When wearing traditional outfits, try to incorporate authentic elements. Research about the culture and its customs to guarantee accuracy and sensitivity. - Casual Clothing: Even when the dress code is relaxed, maintain a neat appearance. Jeans without rips, clean shirts, and appropriate footwear (no flip flops) would be suitable. **Key Tips:** * Always consider comfort along with style. You don't want to be adjusting your clothes constantly during your performance. * Layering can help regulate body temperature and provide versatility. For instance, wearing a cardigan over a sleeveless top gives you the option to remove the outer layer if needed. **Guidelines:** * Refrain from using offensive symbols or messages on your clothes. * Respect religious and cultural sensitivities by avoiding clothing items that might be deemed inappropriate. **Step 3: Accessorize** Accessories can elevate your overall look but remember, less is often more. Keep jewelry minimalistic and classy. Tie back long hair securely so it doesn't obstruct vision or become tangled during performances. Use makeup sparingly focusing mainly on enhancing natural features rather than creating a heavy look. **Key Tips:** - Choose hypoallergenic products to avoid skin reactions especially under hot stage lights. - Test your makeup looks beforehand to ensure it lasts throughout the event. **Step 4: Try Everything On** A few days prior to the concert, try on your entire ensemble including shoes. Walk around, sit down, raise your arms - move as you would during the actual performance. This checks whether everything fits well and feels comfortable. **Step 5: Prepare a Backup Outfit** Despite careful planning, unexpected issues might arise. Having a backup outfit ready can save the day. Select something simple yet matching the dress code just in case. By following these steps, you'll not only look great but also feel at ease, enabling you to focus solely on delivering an outstanding performance at your school concert. The trombone and the triangle have hardly changed for hundreds of years, but the violin family developed from folk instruments such as fiddles and gradually replaced the viols to form the basis of the modern orchestra. Title: How to Add Dolphin Jetpack on Android Dolphin Jetpack is a powerful feature that enhances your browsing experience on the Dolphin browser for Android devices. It provides faster loading times, smoother scrolling, and improved overall performance. This tutorial will guide you through the process of adding Dolphin Jetpack on your Android device with clear and concise steps. Step 1: Check Compatibility Before proceeding, ensure your device meets the compatibility requirements. Your Android device should be running version 4.0 or higher, and have at least 512MB RAM. To check this information, go to Settings > About Phone (or Tablet) > Software Information. Step 2: Download Dolphin Browser If you haven't already installed the Dolphin browser, download it from the Google Play Store by searching 'Dolphin Browser'. Tap 'Install', then 'Accept' when prompted to give permissions. Once installation completes, tap 'Open' to launch the app. Key Tip: Using an up-to-date version of the Dolphin browser ensures optimal functionality and security while browsing. Regularly checking for updates can help prevent potential issues. Step 3: Access Dolphin Settings Tap the three dots icon located at the bottom right corner of the screen to open the menu. Scroll down and select 'Settings'. Step 4: Enable Jetpack In settings, find the section labeled 'Jetpack'. If it isn't enabled yet, toggle the switch beside 'Enable Jetpack'. A popup may appear asking if you want to allow access to photos, media, and files; choose 'Allow'. Guideline: Enabling Jetpack might slightly vary depending on your device model and OS version. However, generally, enabling features involves toggling switches within relevant sections of the application's settings. Step 5: Customize Options (Optional) Under the Jetpack section, there are several options available which can be customized according to personal preference. These include but are not limited to: * Image Quality: Adjust image quality to balance data usage and visual fidelity. * Video Auto-play: Control whether videos start playing automatically upon page load. * Data Saving Mode: Reduce mobile data consumption by compressing web pages before they reach your device. Explore these settings and make adjustments as needed. Remember, changes made here directly impact battery life, internet usage, and user interface responsiveness. Step 6: Test Performance After successfully enabling Dolphin Jetpack, test its effectiveness by visiting some frequently accessed websites or heavy content sites like YouTube, Facebook, etc. Notice improvements in speed, smoothness during scrolling, and general performance boost compared to previous sessions without Jetpack active. Key Tip: Keep in mind that third-party apps, background processes, or outdated software could interfere with expected results. Therefore, maintaining a clean system environment contributes significantly towards achieving optimal performance. By following these simple steps, you now know how to add Dolphin Jetpack on your Android device. Enjoy enhanced browsing experiences characterized by quicker load times, seamless navigation, and better energy efficiency! oh, I thought he said a big something else. Dealing with a codependent family member can be challenging, but understanding the nature of codependency and implementing clear boundaries and self-care strategies can greatly improve your relationship and overall well-being. Here's an in-depth guide to help you navigate this situation: **Step 1: Educate Yourself about Codependency** Codependency is a pattern of behavior where one person (the codependent) enables another person's harmful or irresponsible behaviors while neglecting their own needs and feelings. Understanding this dynamic will allow you to better identify unhealthy patterns in your interactions with your family member. *Key tip*: Recognize that codependency is a complex issue often rooted in past experiences and family dynamics; patience and empathy are crucial as you address these challenges. **Step 2: Identify Unhealthy Patterns** Take note of any recurring negative patterns in your interactions with your codependent family member. These might include excessive caretaking, enabling addictive behaviors, avoiding conflict, or failing to communicate assertively. *Guideline*: Journaling or discussing your observations with a trusted friend or therapist may provide additional insights into these patterns. **Step 3: Establish Clear Boundaries** Communicate your limits clearly and consistently. Let your family member know what behaviors you find unacceptable and define consequences if those boundaries are violated. Remember, setting boundaries isn't about controlling others-it's about taking control of your own actions and reactions. *Key tip*: Be prepared for resistance or pushback when establishing new boundaries. Maintain consistency and reiterate your commitment to maintaining healthy relationships. **Step 4: Practice Assertive Communication** Express your thoughts, feelings, and needs directly and honestly while still considering the other person's perspective. Use "I" statements instead of blaming language, and avoid becoming overly emotional or confrontational during conversations. *Guideline*: Consider role-playing difficult conversations with a trusted friend or therapist before engaging with your codependent family member. This practice can increase confidence and reduce anxiety around assertiveness. **Step 5: Encourage Personal Responsibility** Help your codependent family member recognize the importance of taking responsibility for their own emotions, decisions, and consequences. Avoid rescuing them from natural consequences or making excuses for their behavior. *Key tip*: Supporting personal growth doesn't mean ignoring struggles or hardships. Offer resources and encouragement without assuming responsibility for solving problems. **Step 6: Engage in Self-Care** Prioritize your physical, emotional, and mental health by practicing regular exercise, eating nutritious meals, getting enough sleep, and seeking professional support when needed. Setting aside time for hobbies, relaxation, and social connections also promotes overall well-being. *Guideline*: Develop a consistent self-care routine that includes activities that nurture both your body and mind. **Step 7: Seek Outside Support** Connect with local support groups, online communities, or individual therapy to gain additional guidance and encouragement throughout this process. Sharing your experiences and learning from others who have faced similar challenges can foster a sense of connection and belonging. *Key tip*: Professional therapists trained in addressing codependency issues can offer valuable insight and tailored advice based on your unique circumstances. Remember, dealing with a codependent family member requires ongoing effort, patience, and self-reflection. By following these steps and prioritizing your own well-being, you can create healthier dynamics within your family system. A spirit is considered to be the part of a being that is not the body. Come show me what she what you think she should wear. THE LATE GREAT LADIES OF BLUES AND JAZZ Music is an integral part of daily life. It surrounds us everywhere...at home, in stores, even on the street. Ask your students to list how many places they hear it. Does its all-pervasive presence make it important? Unimportant? Most young people would agree that it is important...at least the music they enjoy. Music captures their imagination and attention. (Just think what would happen if Janet Jackson showed up in your classroom!) THE LATE GREAT LADIES OF BLUES AND JAZZ (in fact, the music of the blues and jazz) may be new to them so making the connection between the popular music they like and where it comes from - namely blues and jazz - may convince them that these musical stars are not strangers. The Student Guide provides a background into the women portrayed in the show. Whatever the style or era, music can be a powerful tool. It can work as a springboard for study and discussion of many topics, musical and otherwise. 1. Ask your students to interview their parents about the kind of music they listened to when they were young. What about a grandparent or elderly friend? They might even discover someone lucky enough to have actually heard Billie Holiday or Dinah Washington. 2. Compare some of today s stars with the "Late Great Ladies". What do they have in common? What makes a star? 3. Bring some recordings to class and discuss the lyrics. For instance: Billie Holiday: "God Bless the Child" Bessie Smith: "Nobody Knows You When You re Down and Out" Ethel Waters: "St. Louis Blues" Dinah Washington: "What a Difference a Day Makes" Mahalia Jackson: "Down by the Riverside" All of these examples are signature songs of the artist available on record or cassette at larger stores such as Tower Records or J & R Music World (both in Manhattan). The Public Library (particularly the branch at Lincoln Center) is likely to have them as well. You may want to ask your students about the music: What do the words mean? How do they make you feel? Do they have relevance today? 1. To learn about jazz and blues is to learn about African-American history. Where does this music come from? What function did music serve in the lives of slaves? For that matter, what function did music serve in African life? 2. Ask your students to research New Orleans as the "Birthplace of Jazz." How did this city obtain such a rich musical heritage? 3, What can students discover about cultural life in Africa, in the American south? 1. What is a tradition? How many can your students think of? Do they have family traditions? Have they changed at all and if so, how? But back to music. If your students ask you to define jazz and blues, good luck! Tell them what the great jazz trumpeter Louis Armstrong said, "If you gotta ask, you ll never know." Then ask them to define the music they like. (They know it when they hear it, right?) If it s so hard to put into words, why is music so important? Music is, in a fundamental way, something we know best of all through experience and feelings, things that are just as important as that which can be easily expressed in words. Through studying music, you can help your students discover their own powers of creative self-expression. Here is some suggested reading for teachers: ALBERTSON, Chris, Bessie: Stein and Day, 1972 DAHL, Linda, Stormy Weather: The Music and Lives of a Century of Jazzwomen: Pantheon books. GOURSE, Leslie, Louis Children: American Jazz Singers: Quill Press, 1984. HOLIDAY, Billie with William Dufty, Lady Sings the Blues: Penquin Books, 1984. And for your students: Cobblestone Magazine, Cobblestone Publishing, Inc. 28 Main Street, Peterborough, NH 03458. Bruce Calhoun, Chairman. The October 1983 issue focuses on jazz and blues. You may also want to look at: Educational Materials Fall/Winter Catalogue 89-90, Learning Arts, PO Box 179, Wichita, KS 67201. Pages 100-103 list a variety of musical resources including films, filmstrips and records. Also, ask your school librarian what she/he might recommend. THE LATE, GREAT LADIES OF BLUES AND JAZZ HOW TO HAVE THE BLUES - AND LOVE IT! Student Guide 1 A Few Notes On The Blues- And All That Jazz Maybe you ve seen a movie called Crossroads, which stars Ralph Maccio of Karate Kid fame. In it, he plays a teenager from Long Island with just one wish: He wants to be a great blues guitarist. He meets up with an old, black "blues: man, and together they travel to New Orleans, Louisiana- the birthplace of jazz and blues music. It s there the old man decides to let Macchio s character in on what jazz and blues are all about. Braced for a lengthy, detailed explanation, he s pretty stunned when the blues man says only: "The blues ain t nothin but a good man feelin bad." But the fact is, that s a more detailed explanation than most real-life jazz and blues musicians are willing to give. The legendary Louis "Louie" Armstrong-called "the epitome of jazz" by a fellow musician-put it this way: "If you gotta ask, you ll never know." The great Thomas Wright "Fats" Waller-who once described himself as "Mrs. Waller s 285 pounds of jam, jive, and everythin"" - put it even more bluntly. Fats said, "If you don t know-don t mess with it." The point is, jazz and blues aren t something you really learn though people telling you about it. It s something you need to experience yourself in order to understand it. And that s not really so strange, if you think about it. It s a lot easier for your to have a friend listen to your new Bon Jovi album than it is trying to explain it. And speaking of Bon Jovi, history shows that if it weren t for blues and jazz, there wouldn t be a Bon Jovi, or a Van Halen, or Cinderella or Poison. That s because to-day s music has its roots in jazz and blues. And as for the roots of jazz and blues...they come from somewhere a little farther away than New Orleans, Louisiana. In fact, they come from a whole different continent: Africa. So how d they Wind Up Here? Most musicologists (a very big word that simply means a person who studies music) believe it was brought over from Africa by the black people who themselves were brought over to the United States to work as slaves. Most also agree it first became popular in New Orleans, around the time of World War 1. Back in those days, there was no such thing as M-TV-probably because there was no such thing as television , period! So this next fact leaves even the musicologists scratching their heads: Jazz and blues caught on quicker than heavy metal-and without benefit or radio or television. To give you an idea of how quickly it caught on, during World War 1, jazz and blues were popular among about 50,000 poor blacks around the New Orleans area, and there were maybe a couple hundred musicians. By 1920, it was popular throughout the whole country. Ten years later, it had performing musicians and fans in Europe as well, and by the time the 1940 s rolled around, jazz and blues were known throughout the world. 20 years later-in 1960-jazz and blues were being hailed as both serious music and art forms. And in 1988, living blues legends like B.B. King-known best for a song you ve probably heard, "The Thrill is Gone"- is still doing sell-out concerts and proving the thrill isn t gone when it comes to jazz and blues. Well, it s like Fats Waller said, "If you don t know-don t mess with it." The only way to know is to experience it-and you will embark on the ultimate, jazz/blues experience through Sandra Reaves-Phillips "The Late, Great Ladies of Blues and Jazz." It s guaranteed that once you meet Ma Rainey (whose outrageous costumes make Madonna look like a n amateur), Ethel Waters (who could out-sing Joan Jett), and Mahalia Jackson (you think Tina Turner wails a tune? Wait), you ll be a member of the jazz/blues fan club. Don t feel bad (if you can t put the experience into words. But if you re pushed to, just remember seven simple words: "If you gotta ask, you ll never know." And tell em Louie Armstrong sent ya. Introducing...Those Late, Great Ladies "Queen of the Blues" Nobody ever said the girl born Gertrude Malissa Prodgett in Columbus, Georgia was pretty. In fact, blues singer and pianist Champion Jack Dupree described her as "a really ugly woman." "But when she opened her mouth, "he continued, "that was it. You forgot everything. What a personality she had!" She became known as "Ma Rainey" when, at age 18, she married comedian, dancer and singer William "Pa" Rainey. The newlyweds put together an act- and literally took it on the road. It was in a small Missouri town that Ma discovered the blues-and from the way it happened, she d most likely agree with the blues man in Crossroads that "the blues ain t nothing but a good man feelin bad." With one minor exception. It was a good woman feelin bad that caught Ma s ear. The way the story goes, a woman showed up outside Ma Rainey s show tent, and started singing a song about a man who d left her. Ma was so impressed with that song, she used it as an encore. To put it simply. ..it knocked the audience s socks off. Ma continued to listen for such songs, and when she heard them she incorporated them into her act. She was the first woman to introduce blues to the public - and she did it with such outrageous style and flair that no one has ever disputed her title of "Mother of the Blues." "Sweet Mama Stringbean"(1900-1977) "Flair" and "style: were about the last things Ethel Waters figured she had. Growing up as a skinny kid in Chester, Pennsylvania, she dreamed of seeing the world by becoming a wealthy white woman s maid. Ethel Waters saw the world, all right. But she didn t need a wealthy white woman to show her. From the minute she stepped on stage in Baltimore- and at age 17, became the first woman ever sing the popular "St. Louis Blues" - to sing the popular "St. Louis Blues"-it was obvious "Sweet Mama Stringbean s" voice was her ticket to the world. She went from Baltimore to Broadway to Hollywood, starring in classic movies such as The Member of the Wedding, On With the Show, and the film version of her successful Broadway hit, Cabin in the Sky. Aside from "St. Louis Blues", she s best remembered for classic blues songs such as "Am I Blue," "Stormy Weather," and "Happiness Is a Thing Called Joe." Her career spanned some fifty years-and in her late years, she moved from singing strictly jazz and blues to more religious-oriented music. Following a concert in the mid-seventies, she s said to have told the audience, "I want to see you all in heaven. Ill be looking for you." She died shortly thereafter-but the high-energy legend of "Sweet Mama Stringbean" is destined to live on in jazz and blues history for ever. (Queen of the Gospel Singers" Mahalia Jackson had a unique sort of problem. She liked to sing gospel music as opposed to blues, explaining: "Anybody singing the blues is in a deep pit yelling for help, and I m simply not in that position." She saw the blues as being...well, blue. As in sad. Her gospel music, on the other hand, was full of hope and life. She started singing it as a little girl in the church her father preached at in New Orleans. But as was the case with Ethel Waters, Mahalia s voice would prove to be her ticket to the world. She began singing with well-known gospel groups-and before she knew it, the girl from New Orleans was performing in England, France and Denmark-and entertaining rather important folks such as the Empress of Japan, the Prime Minister of India, as well as former Presidents Dwight D. Eisenhower and John F. Kennedy. In 1963, Mahalia sang on the steps of the Lincoln Memorial in Washington, D.C. just prior to Rev. Martin Luther King s famous "I Have a Dream" speech. And in between, Mahalia bent just enough to please jazz/blues fans by recording more "pop" oriented music with then-popular orchestras, such as Duke Ellington s. But it was never at the expense of not pleasing herself. One night Mahalia might bring in as much as $10,000 for a concert promoter; the next night, she would be found in some small-town church, belting out her famous voice for free. How come? Mahalia herself probably said it best. "I don t sing for money," she once explained. "I sing because I love to sing." Question: These are the questions that my music teacher asked me and I didn't quite know what he was trying to tell me. Here are the questions: - Of what is the major scale composed? - What degrees of the scale are used to build the major scale? Could you help?Courtney Albert's reply: All scales are composed of intervals. Standard scales (major and minor) are all composed only of seconds; there are no thirds, fourths or any other intervals involved. The interval between any of these notes and the next is a second: Since we don't have a clef, we don't know what notes these actually are-only that the intervals between them are seconds. We can ascertain this purely visually, since subsequent notes are all on the very next available line or space above the last. Since we don't know the actual notes (only their relative positions to one another), we don't know what kind of seconds they are. Each second can be diminished, minor, major or augmented (each of these is one half step greater than the last.) Intervals can be abbreviated as follows: The major scale is composed of the following intervals: M2, M2, m2, M2, M2, M2, m2. There are seven notes in the major (and minor) scale, and they are referred to by scale degrees 1 through 7. The first note of the scale is scale degree 1, the second is scale degree 2, etc. For more detail, please read How to Play a Piano Scale. For example, names of days and months are considered proper names in English, but not in Spanish, French, Swedish, Slovenian or Finnish, where they are not capitalized. Problem C6 addresses the idea of parallel lines. You might want to refer to the graphs drawn in Part B. Ask if lines with the same slope ever intersect. Students and teachers often miss the essential connection between solving linear equations and finding the intersection of lines. The intersection of two lines happens at an (x, y) pair that satisfies both linear equations. At this point in each equation, the xs are equal and the ys are equal. To find where this happens, set the ys equal to each other and solve each equation for x. When trying to solve an equation like 5x = 5x + 25 by the usual method of "doing the same thing to both sides," students end up with the equation 0 = 25 and don't know how to interpret it. The point is that the two lines are parallel, so they never intersect, which means there is no solution to the equation. And the equation 0 = 25 is never true. Solving equations is covered more completely in the next session, but if there is time, think about some of these ideas now. << back to Part C: Rates In developing countries, diarrhea is also one of the most common causes of malnutrition in children under age 5. These chemicals cause inflammation in a part of the body. When a germ makes someone sick, the body shows it. If a bacteria gets under someone's skin and causes an infection, the skin gets red, hot, and painful. This redness, heat, and pain are signs of "inflammation". This shows that WBCs are fighting the infection and killing the bacteria. That's right said Raggedy Ann with a big smile. Once upon a time, there was a little girl who loved exploring. Title: How to Groom a Horse in a Stable Grooming your horse is an essential part of horsemanship that promotes the health and well-being of your animal companion. Regular grooming helps maintain their coat's shine, prevents hair matting and tangling, stimulates blood circulation, and strengthens the bond between you and your horse. Here is our comprehensive guide on grooming a horse in a stable. **Things You'll Need:** 1. Curry comb or rubber mitt 2. Body brush 3. Mane and tail comb 4. Hoof pick 5. Shedding blade (optional) 6. Soft finishing brush 7. Towel 8. Water and sponge (for muddy areas) 9. Fly spray (seasonal) ### **Step 1: Prepare Your Grooming Tools** Before starting the grooming process, gather all necessary tools and ensure they are clean and in good condition. Dirty brushes can introduce bacteria and cause skin irritation; damaged bristles may harm your horse. Clean your brushes regularly by soaking them in warm water with mild dish soap, then rinse thoroughly and let air dry. ### **Step 2: Find a Quiet Space** Choose a quiet area within the stable where both you and your horse feel comfortable. A calm environment reduces stress and anxiety for both parties. Make sure there is enough space around your horse to move freely without bumping into walls or other objects. ### **Step 3: Secure the Horse** Securely tie your horse using a quick-release knot to a sturdy post or ring at a height appropriate for easy grooming. Always use soft ropes or lead lines to avoid causing injury. Never leave your horse unattended while tied up. ### **Step 4: Begin with Currying** Start by currying your horse using a curry comb or rubber mitt. This action loosens dirt, dead hair, and debris from the coat. Apply gentle pressure in circular motions, working against the direction of the hair growth. Focus on larger muscle groups such as the neck, shoulders, hindquarters, and barrel but avoid bony areas like the spine, face, and legs. ### **Step 5: Use a Body Brush** After currying, switch to a stiff body brush to remove the loose hair and dirt collected during currying. Brush in the same pattern - against the hair growth - following the same sequence used when currying. Be cautious around sensitive areas like the eyes, ears, and genitals. ### **Step 6: Smooth Out Coat** Next, use a soft finishing brush to smooth out the coat and restore its natural texture. Brush along the direction of hair growth this time, making long strokes to distribute oils evenly across the coat. Pay special attention to the mane and tail, detangling gently with care not to pull out too much hair. ### **Step 7: Comb Manes and Tails** = = = children/CHIDES_NA/NewmanRatner/18/6314AK.cha = = = Some statements are always true. That kind of statement is called tautology"." One popular classical tautology, credited to the philosopher Parmenides of Elea, says "That which is, is. That which is not, is not." This essentially means that true statements are true and false statements are false. Due to their simple nature, tautologies may not always be helpful in building logical arguments. Title: How to Change Your Screen Saver on a Samsung Cell Phone Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of changing your screensaver on a Samsung cell phone. A screensaver is a visual representation that appears when your device is idle or locked for an extended period. It serves as a protective measure to prevent screen burn-in and offers personalization options for users. By following these steps, you'll be able to customize your Samsung device with a preferred screensaver. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Make sure your Samsung cell phone is updated to the latest software version before proceeding. This ensures compatibility with all features and functions, including screensavers. * Be mindful of battery life; prolonged display of animations can consume more power than static images. * Remember, this tutorial focuses specifically on Samsung devices. Other Android phones may have slightly different procedures. Steps to change your screensaver: Step 1: Unlock your device and open the "Settings" app. The Settings app provides access to various configuration options available on your Samsung cell phone. To locate it quickly, pull down the notification panel from the top of the home screen and tap on the gear icon. If unavailable there, look for the app drawer (usually represented by a grid of dots) at the bottom of the home screen and scroll until you find the Settings app. Step 2: Scroll down and select "Display". Within the Display settings menu, you can modify several aspects related to your screen appearance and behavior. Here, we aim to adjust the screensaver settings. Step 3: Tap on "Screen saver". Inside the Display settings, search for the option labeled "Screen saver". Selecting this will take you directly to where you can manage and configure your device's screensaver. Step 4: Choose your desired screensaver type. Samsung typically offers multiple categories of screensavers such as Clock, Color fill, Photo table, and others depending upon the model. Explore each category and preview their respective styles by tapping them once. Once satisfied with a choice, move forward. Step 5: Customize additional settings if applicable. For certain screensaver types, further customizations might be possible. For instance, selecting 'Clock' could allow you to pick between digital or analog clock designs, choose colors, font sizes, etc. Modify accordingly based on preference then hit 'Done'. Step 6: Set the delay time. Underneath the selected screensaver, set the duration after which the screensaver should activate. Slide the timer left or right to increase or decrease the waiting period respectively. Keep in mind longer delays save energy but also mean less frequent displays of your chosen screensaver. Step 7: Confirm changes. After setting up everything according to your liking, ensure all modifications are saved correctly. Typically, no explicit 'Save' button exists; therefore, simply navigating away implies automatic saving. However, double-check just to be safe. Conclusion: Congratulations! You've successfully changed your screensaver on a Samsung cell phone. Personalizing your device adds charm and uniqueness reflective of your personality. Enjoy exploring other customizable features within your Samsung device too! A similar class of vehicle is the CUV crossover, a common UK term. That is built on a car chassis. Often it uses a Unibody chassis instead of the heavier body-on-frame design of SUVs. Crossover vehicles often have all-wheel-drive instead of four-wheel drive. Crossovers are usually lighter than SUVs and get better fuel mileage. In general, when referring to an SUV, many include crossovers. However it is incorrect to refer to an SUV on a truck frame as a crossover. Title: How to Chop Mint Leaves Like a Pro Introduction: Mint leaves are a popular herb used in various dishes and beverages around the world. They have a refreshing taste that can elevate your culinary creations. To get the most out of mint leaves, you need to chop them correctly. This tutorial will provide an in-depth guide on how to chop mint leaves with precision and efficiency. Materials Needed: 1. Fresh mint leaves 2. A sharp knife (a chef's knife or paring knife would work best) 3. Cutting board 4. Bowl (to collect chopped mint leaves) Step 1: Select Your Mint Leaves Key Tip: Choose bright green, crisp leaves as they yield the best flavor and aroma when chopped. Avoid yellowish or wilted ones. Explanation: The freshness of the mint leaf significantly impacts its taste and smell. Older leaves may lack the intensity required for certain recipes. Step 2: Rinse & Dry the Mint Leaves Key Guideline: Gently rinse the mint leaves under cold running water to remove any dirt or impurities. Pat dry using a clean kitchen towel or let them air dry completely before proceeding. Explanation: Moisture on the surface of the leaves could make your cutting board slippery, increasing the risk of injury while chopping. Also, wet leaves tend to clump together, making uniform chopping difficult. Step 3: Prepare Your Workspace Place the dried mint leaves on your cutting board. Make sure the board is stable and won't move during the process. Keep your bowl nearby to transfer the chopped mint directly. Step 4: Hold the Knife Correctly Hold the handle firmly with your dominant hand, keeping your fingers curled away from the blade. Use your other hand to steady the base of the knife near the bolster (the part where the blade meets the handle). Step 5: Stack the Leaves Stack about five to six mint leaves atop one another. This makes it easier to cut through multiple leaves simultaneously, saving time and ensuring consistent sizing. Step 6: Start Chopping Begin by slicing vertically down the stack of leaves. Try not to squash the leaves; instead, aim for thin, neat cuts. After reaching the bottom of the pile, slide the uncut portion towards the cut side so all layers face downwards. Repeat this until the leaves are finely chopped. Step 7: Rotate and Continue Chopping Turn the pile 90 degrees and repeat the vertical cuts horizontally across the previous direction. This crosswise chopping ensures even smaller pieces. Step 8: Transfer Chopped Mint Using the tip of your knife, push the chopped mint into the waiting bowl. Be careful not to include any unchopped bits. If needed, give the mixture a final quick chop before transferring. Conclusion: Now you know how to chop mint leaves like a pro! Remember, practice makes perfect - don't worry if your first few attempts aren't flawless. With patience and repetition, you'll soon master this essential cooking skill. Enjoy adding that burst of freshness to your meals! I will remember said Little Red Riding Hood. Where'd you go last week in school? [showing Adam how to perform coin trick] Uses of Perl About the Site To get information from someone looking at a homepage to a program on the server, there has to be some way to enter the information. The thing that does this is called forms. Forms are a set of fields, places where information can be entered, that after submission, the information is sent to the server and a program processes it. In this lesson, we will concentrate on the forms and how to make them. First, here is a sample form, in which you would enter your name, and then submit it. Now the explanation: <form></form> : The form tag tells the browser that the enclosed text is a form. You must make sure that you close the form for it to work completely. <...action="cgi-bin/name.cgi"> : The action attribute tells the browser what program will process the information when submitted. <...method="post"> : The method attribute tells the browser how to send the information. There are two methods, get and post. This is not really important now, but you will learn more about this later. <input...> : This tag tells the browser to display an input field so that you can enter text. The type of input field is important, because each one does a different job. The text type lets you enter in text, but only into a one line box. The submit type is a button which you press to send the information to the server and the program named in the action. Here are the properties for the most important <input...> : Here are the most important properties for the <...type="..."> inputs: To use these properties, insert (for example) the name "hello" into the <input> tag. There are different types of math transformation, one of which is the type y = f(bx). This type of math transformation is a horizontal compression when b is greater than one. We can graph this math transformation by using tables to transform the original elementary function. Other important transformations include vertical shifts, horizontal shifts, and reflections. Let's investigate another transformation. I want to know what's the transformation y=f of bx do? So let's start with the function f of x =4-x I'm going to graph that function and then I want to graph f of 4x. So let's make this our parent function it's actually pretty easy to come up with values for it and you know what the shape is going to be, it's a radical function. So I'll make this u and root 4-u, let's pick values to plug in that'll give us nice perfect squares inside the radical. So for example u=0 is a good choice because it gives you 4-0, 4 and 4 is 2. If I want to get 1 inside here I'd pick u=3, so I'll pick 3 I get 1 inside and the square root of that is 1 and if I want to get 0 I'll pick u=4. So 4-4 is 0, 0 is 0, so these are the three points I'll use to graph it. And let me graph it right now 0 2, 3 1, 4 0 and you can see that if it's a radical function. This is it's end point so it's going to open to the left and it'll look something like this, now what does the transformation do? Well let me make a substitution, I want to graph f of 4x so let me make the substitution, first of all this is y equals the square root of 4-4x right? f of 4x is replacing the x by 4x, so I'll substitute u for 4x. Now if u equals 4x that means x equals one quarter u. That's why I take these u values and I multiply them by a quarter. And I get 0 times a quarter is 0, 3 times a quarter is three quarters and four times a quarters 1. These are my x values, and then here I'll have root 4-4x and this would be exactly the same as this because 4x is u, so 4-u exactly these values 2, 1, 0. So the old end point which is way out here has moved in to here, and this is what my new graph looks like. Title: How to Find Exact Values for Trigonometric Functions ========================================================= Step 1: Understand the Basics of Trigonometry ---------------------------------------------- Trigonometry is the study of triangles and their relationships with angles. The three primary trigonometric functions are sine (sin), cosine (cos), and tangent (tan). These functions relate the ratios of the sides of a right triangle to one of its acute angles. For this tutorial, we will focus on finding exact values for these trigonometric functions using special right triangles and unit circles. ### Key Tips and Guidelines * Remember that sine, cosine, and tangent are functions, meaning they take an input (an angle) and return a specific output related to that angle's position within a triangle or circle. * Familiarize yourself with basic terminology such as hypotenuse, opposite side, adjacent side, and the Pythagorean theorem. Step 2: Learn About Special Right Triangles ------------------------------------------- Special right triangles have specific angle measurements that allow you to find exact trigonometric function values without needing a calculator. There are two main special right triangles: the 45-45-90 degree triangle and the 30-60-90 degree triangle. **45-45-90 Degree Triangle:** This triangle has equal leg lengths and a hypotenuse that is longer than either leg by $\sqrt{2}$. Since both legs are congruent, any ratio involving just one leg and half the hypotenuse will be equivalent to $1/\sqrt{2}$ which simplifies to $\boxed{\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}}$. ![45-45-90 triangle](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/aa/Find-Exact-Values-for-Trigonometric-Functions- [points to the book as if to show MOT something] Backing off fifteen or twenty feet from the bole of the tree beneath the branches of which Tarzan worked upon his rope, Gazan scampered quickly forward, scrambling nimbly upward to the lower limbs. Here he would squat for a moment or two, quite proud of his achievement, then clamber to the ground again and repeat. Sometimes, quite often in fact, for he was an ape, his attention was distracted by other things, a beetle, a caterpillar, a tiny field mouse, and off he would go in pursuit; the caterpillars he always caught, and sometimes the beetles; but the field mice, never. Now he discovered the tail of the rope upon which Tarzan was working. Grasping it in one small hand he bounced away, for all the world like an animated rubber ball, snatching it from the ape-man's hand and running off across the clearing. Types of Prime Numbers There are several types of primes, however there are two main ones. These are Fermat primes, and Mersenne primes. Each is described as two to the power of a number, and then plus or minus one. The format for Fermat primes is Fn= 22^n + 1. One example of this would be- Three is a prime number. Some other Fermat primes are 5 and 17. There is only a limited number of Fermat primes, and not every number put into this equation turns out to be prime. Mersenne primes can be expressed as Mn = 2n - 1. Here's an example Mn =4 -1 Three is a prime number, and it is unique because it is both a Mersenne prime and a Fermat prime. There are many more Mersenne primes, such as 7 and 31. Many very large prime numbers have been found using these formulas. In fact, the largest known prime (as of May 1st, 2007) is 232582657 -1. Many of the largest primes found are mersenne primes, because they are easy to check for primality with a computer. As I mentioned, there are many other types of primes, such as twin primes, Wieferich primes, Wall-Sun-Sun primes, Wilson primes, and Wolstenholme primes. Solving Math Word Problems on the ACT Part of the ACT Math For Dummies Cheat Sheet A word problem (also called a story problem or a problem in a setting) gives you information in words rather than in just equations and numbers. To answer a math word problem on the ACT, you have to translate the provided information into one or more equations and then solve. You can solve some word problems fairly easily. Jotting down the numbers in the problem can be useful to help get you focused and moving in the right direction. The following example word problem shows you how: A charity is holding a lottery to raise money. A book of 20 tickets sells for $70.00, and a book of 50 tickets sells for $150.00. How much do you save on each ticket by buying a book of 50 tickets rather than a book of 20 tickets? If you're not immediately sure how to proceed, jot down the numbers in an orderly fashion: Book of 50 $150 50 Book of 20 $70 20 This step only takes a moment and gets your brain moving. When you organize the information in this way, you may see that the next step involves division: Book of 50 $150 50 = $3.00 Book of 20 $70 20 = $3.50 Now you can easily see that buying a book of 50 tickets saves $0.50 per ticket, so the correct answer is Choice (D). One Hundred Hungry Ants by Elinor J. Pinczes Number and Operation Arrays, multiplication, division Lesson Created by: Tracy Tauschek, Woodrow Wilson Elementary, Manhattan, KS - Book One Hundred Hungry Ants by Elinor J. Pinczes, grid paper, unifix cubes, pencils, markers or crayons, lined paper, and blank paper. 1. Launching the Lesson - Instruct the students to listen and look for math while you are reading the book. - Read the book. - Ask the students what parts of the story remind them of the math you have been doing in class. Discuss how the book contains arrays of ants. We have used arrays in class to show multiplication and division problems. What other arrays could we find in real life? 2. Developing the Lesson - How many different arrays does this book have? Go back through the book, stopping at each new array. Ask the students what the array is, what multiplication and division problems does it show? - Tell the students they will be rewriting this book. They will get to pick an animal and number to use in their book. Their books need to have at least four different arrays. Give out grid paper and unifix cubes so the students can start brainstorming arrays. Step one is to choose a number and at least four arrays. Step two choose an animal and where the animal is going or what it is trying to do. After that they may begin writing a rough draft of their story. The title should have alliteration. - Students could peer edit each others rough drafts. Then create the final copy complete with illustrations. - Have the students read the completed books to each other or book buddies. Discuss after reading the book aloud what could they have done differently or changed to make it better. - Students could help younger students rewrite the book. - Students could make their books into power point presentations. Title: How to Convert DVDs to Play on Your iPhone 4 Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of converting DVD movies to a format compatible with your iPhone 4. This can be useful when you want to watch your favorite DVD films on-the-go without carrying physical discs. By following this step-by-step guide, you'll learn about required tools, settings adjustments, and best practices. What You Need: 1. A computer (Windows or Mac) 2. A DVD drive (internal or external) 3. DVD ripping software (HandBrake recommended for both Windows and Mac users) 4. iTunes installed on your computer 5. An iPhone 4 with sufficient storage space 6. USB cable to connect your iPhone 4 to your computer **Step 1:** **Install Necessary Software** First, download and install Handbrake from their official website (<https://handbrake.fr/>). For Mac users, ensure that you also have macOS' built-in utility called "Disk Utility" which is used during the conversion process later on. *Key Tip:* Always check if the version you are downloading is up-to-date and legitimate. Avoid any third-party sites offering free versions as they may contain malware. **Step 2:** **Insert the DVD** Put the DVD into your computer's optical drive or an external one connected via USB. Wait until the disc fully loads before proceeding. **Step 3:** **Open Handbrake** Launch Handbrake. It should automatically detect the inserted DVD. If not, click on "Source" button at top left corner, then select "Open DVD Folder" and navigate to your DVD folder. *Guideline:* Be patient while Handbrake scans the DVD; larger files might take longer to load. **Step 4:** **Choose Output Destination** Click "Browse..." next to "Save As:" field under "Destination" section. Choose where you want to save the converted file once completed. Name the file appropriately so you know what movie it contains. **Step 5:** **Select Device & Preset** From the "Output Settings," choose 'iPhone & iPod Touch.' Then, pick the preset according to your preference - either 'Normal' or 'High Profile.' The latter provides better quality but results in a bigger file size. **Step 6:** **Start Ripping Process** Press "Start" button located near the top. Depending on your system specifications and the length of the film, this could take anywhere from several minutes to over an hour. *Key Tips:* + Don't interrupt the power supply or close Handbrake prematurely during the ripping process. + Keep other resource-intensive tasks minimal to speed up the process. **Step 7:** **Check Converted File** Once done, locate the newly created MP4 file in the specified directory. Open iTunes and drag this video onto its interface. Make sure your library window is active while doing this. **Step 8:** **Sync With iPhone 4** Connect your iPhone 4 using its USB cable. Click on your device icon within iTunes. Go to "Movies" tab, tick "Sync Movies," then select the recently added movie(s). Finally, hit "Apply" followed by "Sync." After syncing completes, disconnect your iPhone 4. Now, enjoy watching your DVD collection directly from your mobile device! Because I already know what her room like looks like. Today, let's talk about something cool called "Brownian Motion." You might think it has something to do with brown colors or moving in a browser, but it's actually about tiny particles moving around in a liquid or gas! Imagine you drop a single speck of dust or a small grain of pollen into a glass of water. Have you ever noticed how that little particle moves randomly, sometimes going up, then down, left, and right, without stopping? That's because water molecules are constantly bumping into it, making it move unpredictably. This kind of motion is called Brownian Motion, named after a scientist called Robert Brown who first observed it under his microscope. Now imagine having many, many more of these tiny particles - so many that you couldn't possibly keep track of each one individually. But together, all those wiggly movements create a sort of fuzzy cloud that spreads out and fills the space over time. And even though you can't see the individual particles anymore, scientists can still describe their overall behavior using math formulas! One interesting thing about Brownian Motion is that it helps us understand some basic ideas behind probability and statistics. Just like flipping coins or rolling dice, when lots of random things happen together, certain patterns start to emerge. By studying these patterns, researchers learn more about not just the particles themselves, but also the liquids and gases they're swimming in. So next time you see foggy air, or watch grains of sand shifting on a beach, remember - you're witnessing Brownian Motion in action! Neat, huh? no, the man was holding the monkey. This is a new game you found, huh? Here is the first week of my Shapes Unit. These are just the problems--I do lots and lots of other activities to get the kids exploring and really thinking about the attributes of shapes. I will detail specific activities tomorrow in a warm-up post. But for now, here are some problems! This is a very hands-on unit. I provide my students with lots pattern blocks and other shape tiles so that they can explore and manipulate shapes in a very concrete manner. For this problem, I give each student a blackline master of a circle and some colored square tiles. I challenge them to fill the circle completely with the square tiles. They cannot overlap the tiles and they must cover the circle completely--no white spaces left. They will try and try and try--but despite their best efforts, they are unable to complete the challenge. The most important part of this problem is the discussion that occurs during and afterwards. Why can't they fill the circle with the square tiles? They will inevitably come to the conclusion that they cannot fill the circle with squares because squares have straight edges and circles have a round edge. I then challenge the students to use the square tiles to see what shapes they are able to make. They quickly discover that they can make different sizes of rectangles and squares. And they try and try to make triangles...and some think they actually do. This leads to some more really good discussion...Can you make a triangle? Why or why not? Of course the answer is no--and the reasons are a little too complicated for kindergartners to understand (the angles of a triangle must equal 180 degrees and the angles of a square are 90 degrees, which means you could only have 2 angles and a triangle needs 3...and even my head is about to explode right now!) The purpose of this activity is for the kids to explore and wonder...they will start to see that the shape of a square's corners do not fit in a triangle's corners (unless it's a right triangle). They are building a schema upon which they will add to in the coming years as they learn more and more about geometry! This is another activity similar to Day 1. I provide the kids with a blackline master of a triangle and pattern blocks. I challenge them to fill the triangle. The same rules apply--you cannot overlap shapes and there can be no white spaces remaining. I give them all the pattern blocks--even the orange squares and the skinny white parallelograms that won't fit. I let them try and try until they figure out that, because of the shape of the corners (the angles), they won't fit. Again--they are building that schema! I give the kids paper pattern blocks and have them glue them into their math journals. I also have them fill out a chart with the number of each shape they used. For share time, we take a gallery walk around the room to see the different combinations of shapes the kids used. This activity is based on the Investigations Fill the Hexagon game. I give the students a blackline master with several hexagons on it and pattern blocks. I challenge them to fill the hexagons in as many different ways as they can. Again--I let them figure out that the orange square and skinny white parallelogram aren't going to fit. For share time, we make a class chart that shows all the different ways the kids were able to fill the hexagons... Here's a fun problem that the kids love. I actually hide 2 shapes in a basket and they get to guess what shapes they are. It is essential that kids have actual shapes to manipulate and count the sides when solving this problem. Most will not use an addition strategy at this point in the year...they will simply guess and check. They will choose 2 shapes and count the sides. If they do not equal 7, they will try again. It is interesting to see which kids will put both shapes back and start all over again from scratch, and which will keep one shape. Watch for the kids that figure out that they have too many or too few sides and choose their second shape accordingly. I have the kids record their answers in their math journals. For share time, we make a class chart recording the different combinations of shapes that have 7 sides. This year, one of my kids figured out that "the triangle always stays the same." He has very good computational fluency, and figured out that it was going to have to be a combination of 3 and 4 sides, and that a triangle is the only shape that has 3 sides! When we are all done, I open up the basket and reveal the shapes inside--the kids love to see whether or not they were right! So this is the same as yesterday's problem, but taking it a bit further--this time I do not tell them how many shapes I have--just that it's "some." So there are even more possible combinations. Again, during share time, we make a chart with the different combinations the kids thought of. During the big reveal, the kids discovered that I had included an oval, which added zero sides--they loved that! Do you think you've got a bad deal in this house? Title: How to Deal With Acid Rain Acid rain is a type of precipitation that has a pH level less than 5.6, making it more acidic than normal rainwater. This can have harmful effects on both the environment and human health. Fortunately, there are steps you can take to mitigate its impact. Here's a comprehensive guide on dealing with acid rain: **Step 1: Understand What Causes Acid Rain** Acid rain primarily results from emissions of sulfur dioxide (SO2) and nitrogen oxides (NOX), which react in the atmosphere with water, oxygen, and other chemicals to form various acidic compounds. The main sources of these pollutants are burning fossil fuels such as coal, oil, or natural gas by power plants, industrial processes, and vehicles. Key Tip: Knowing the cause helps us focus our efforts where they matter most - reducing pollution at their source. **Step 2: Monitor Local Air Quality Indexes** Stay informed about air quality indexes in your area. Many environmental agencies provide real-time data online or through mobile apps. Poor air quality often indicates higher levels of pollutants contributing to acid rain. Guideline: Regularly checking these indices will help you understand when precautions may be necessary. **Step 3: Protect Your Property** If you live in an area prone to acid rain, consider using materials resistant to acidic conditions for construction or renovation projects. For example, stainless steel, concrete, and certain types of stone tend to resist corrosion better than others. Key Tip: Preventive measures like this can save costly repairs down the line. **Step 4: Maintain Your Vehicles Properly** Regular vehicle maintenance reduces exhaust gases, thereby minimizing contribution to acid rain. Regular oil changes, tire rotations, and timely repairs ensure efficient fuel combustion and lower emissions. Guideline: Follow manufacturer recommendations for servicing intervals. **Step 5: Use Clean Energy Sources** Switching to cleaner energy alternatives like solar, wind, or hydroelectric power decreases demand for electricity generated from fossil fuels, thus helping reduce SO2 and NOX emissions. Key Tip: Every bit counts; even small changes can make a difference. **Step 6: Advocate for Policy Changes** Lobby local government representatives to enforce stricter regulations on industries emitting high levels of SO2 and NOX. Support policies encouraging renewable energy adoption and promoting public transportation to decrease reliance on private vehicles. Guideline: Change starts locally; influencing policy can lead to widespread improvements. **Step 7: Educate Others** Share knowledge about acid rain with family, friends, colleagues, and community members. Encourage them to adopt eco-friendly practices and support initiatives working towards cleaner environments. Key Tip: Education sparks action; informing others spreads awareness and fosters collective responsibility. Remember, while we cannot completely stop acid rain, individual actions collectively contribute to significant reductions in pollutant levels, leading to healthier ecosystems and communities. By following these steps, you're playing your part in preserving our planet for future generations. how's the birdie in the clock go? First we height put the motor in, right? But having only a franc or two in my pocket, or indeed in the world, I did not know how to procure the means of going forward; and whilst I was lounging about the place, forming first one plan and then another, I saw you in the church, and concluding you were in pursuit of me, I thought the best way of eluding your vigilance was to make my way back to Paris as fast as I could; so I set off instantly, and walked all the way; but having no money to pay my night's lodging, I came here to borrow a couple of livres of my sister Claudine, who lives in the fifth story." By 1860, almost 1/3 of the population was made up of slaves in the south. Most slaves worked on cotton plantations. The men and women planted, harvested, and removed weeds and other unwanted plants from the land. Teenagers worked in the fields, too. Work for children wasn t too hard. They pulled out weeds, picked insects off of the crops, and took water to other workers. Slaves lives didn t matter to anyone. An owner of a slave could do anything to them. They thought of slaves as items you buy instead of human beings. If a slave didn t work hard, their owner usually didn t treat their slave well. Some owners treated their slaves well, so they would do good work because slaves were very expensive. Some people did not treat slaves well. They sometimes beat slaves, and they also punished them very badly. The main reason for this is because the owners had complete power over their slaves, and they thought their slaves would work harder if they were afraid of being punished by their owners. The slaves worked very hard, but the owners did not pay them. The owners considered them to be property (not people) that they could treat as badly as they wanted. It was hard to keep a family together for slaves. This was because when slaves got sold, they would most likely get split up and would have to go to different plantations to work. Once a family member was sold away to another plantation, they sometimes would never see their family again. Many slaves wanted to visit their families, but they were too far away or they may not have even known where their family was. Some baby slaves didn t even know their family because some of them were sold right after they were born. To keep the slaves under control, the people of the south made laws about what slaves could and could not do. The slaves could not do the following: Enslaved African Americans responded in different ways to punishments. Many slaves tried to run away to the north or even Canada. They were usually caught. One of the most daring things that slaveholders were afraid of was when the slaves fought back with weapons. Nat Turner was an abolitionist. He killed many white people because they abused slaves. One person he killed was his owner because Turner was a slave, and his owner treated him badly. Turner led a group of people to help him kill some slave owners because he wanted to get back at them for owning and abusing slaves. This was called a revolt. Nat Turner s mother strongly hated slavery. He was taught to read by his owner s son. Because of the hatred of slavery in his family, Turner started to believe that he was the one chosen by god to lead people out of slavery. Shortly after Turner was sold to the Travis family, there was an eclipse. The eclipse convinced Turner that it was a sign from god for him to start a rebellion. A while after the eclipse, Turner and 60 to 70 other slaves killed 60 white people in Virginia. Turner s owner s family was one of the white families that were killed. More white people were killed during Turner s rebellion than any other rebellion in history. The Virginia Militia caught 20 of the slaves. They were hanged along with Turner. Angry white people killed over 100 innocent slaves because of their anger over Turner s rebellion. The rebellion caused the Southerners to pass strict laws about slaves having under control. Nat Turner is remembered today because he stood up for his individual rights. He believed that other people shouldn t own people. He also thought that everyone should have freedom, not just certain people. There were many other slaves that succeeded in revolts, but there were also many who did not. The first major slave revolt in the South was led by Gabriel Prosser, a twenty-four year old slave. Prosser wanted to take the city of Richmond, Virginia by force. Prosser also wanted to arm his rebel slaves. Soon, Prosser had thousands of slaves that had joined him, and he had many weapons. On the day of the revolt, everyone was ready to attack Richmond, but a flood in Richmond destroyed the bridges. The next day, the state militia attacked him, and he and his soldiers were hanged. Slaves had many consequences for trying to rebel. The most common ones were getting killed or thrown into jail. There were many other consequences for trying to rebel that were very painful like getting beaten or whipped. Racist white people acted as though black slaves were not people and as if they came from a different world. What white people didn t see in black slaves is that they were the same as them. Not all white people used to treat black slaves badly, but slavery was the practice of owning another human. Many slave owners believed that they could treat their slaves worse than farm animals because the slaves were their property to treat however they wished. Gatell, Frank Otto. "Turner, Nat." After her father's death, Virginia and her brothers and sister moved to Bloomsbury, London and there they became members of the famous group of artists called Bloomsbury Group. Here Virginia met writer Leonard Woolf (1880-1969), whom she was married to from 1912 until her death. Together, they moved to Richmond, where they opened a publishing office called Hogarth House Press, which later published all of Virginia's novels and essays. Disable Hearing Aid Mode on an iPhone: A Comprehensive Step-by-step Guide Hearing aid mode is a feature available on iPhones that enhances audio quality when using hearing aids. However, if you don't need this feature or are experiencing issues with your phone's sound, you may want to disable it. This guide will walk you through the process of disabling hearing aid mode on your iPhone in simple, easy-to-follow steps. Step 1: Open the Settings App The first step is to open the settings app on your iPhone. You can do this by locating the gray gear icon on your home screen or in your app library and tapping on it. The settings app allows you to customize various aspects of your device, including accessibility features like hearing aid mode. Step 2: Navigate to Accessibility Settings Once you have opened the settings
